title: Frontier AI On Your Laptop.
description:
In this video, we will look at DS-4, that enables DeepSeek V4 Flash to run on a 128GB unified memory. 

LINK: https://github.com/antirez/ds4
Blogpost: https://engineerprompt.ai/writing/dwarfstar-4/

My voice to text App: whryte.com
Website: https://engineerprompt.ai/
RAG Beyond Basics Course:
https://prompt-s-site.thinkific.com/courses/rag
Signup for Newsletter, localgpt:
https://tally.so/r/3y9bb0

Let's Connect: 
🦾 Discord: https://discord.com/invite/t4eYQRUcXB
☕ Buy me a Coffee: https://ko-fi.
raw_transcript: Okay, so is unified memory good for running capable models? This is Deep Seek V4 Flash. 284 billion parameter model, one of the most capable open weight model that we have seen. Now, this model shouldn't fit on any of these devices. Memory requirement for this, stored the normal way, is almost four times what these devices have. But, you can still run this device, but it runs on both of them at real usable speed. And how it runs is one of the most interesting piece of system engineerings that I have seen this year. This is a project called Dwarf Star from the creator of Redis. Let me draw exactly how this works. Okay, so let's start with this problem. Small language models have gotten really good. You can run a 1 billion or an 8 billion model on basically anything now. They're fast, they're private, they're free. And for most of the tasks, they're genuinely all you need. But, here's the uncomfortable truth. Our capability still scales with size. The bigger models are simply better at reasoning, at coding, at tool usage. Now, Deep Seek V4 Flash is really good at agentic coding tasks. This is what we would call a quasi frontier performance from an open weight model. The problem is that the models worth running are exactly the ones you can't run. They live in data centers behind APIs, even though if they are open weight models. So, the question is how do you get a model like that onto hardware that you actually own? Okay, but first let's talk about why exactly they don't fit. So, if you have a 284 billion parameters stored each one at 16 bit, you need 568 GB just for the weights. Now, a maxed out MacBook Pro or DGX Spark gives you 128 GB. So, we are not close. Now, even if you cut every weight to eight bits, you still need 284 GB, which is more than double the VRAM that these devices have. And that's actually the main issue with local models. It's kind of binary. Either the model fits in memory or it doesn't run at all. So, if you're using a hosted version of open weight models, you're still accessing it through an API. And basically, it's the same as paying for OpenAI or Anthropic. Now, the standard escape hatch is quantization. And to understand what Dwarf Star does differently, you need to see what quantization actually does. So, every weight in the neural network is just a number. Most of them tiny values scattered around zero. At 16 bits, you have 65,000 possible values to store each one, which is plenty of precision. Now, quantization says, "Use fewer levels." At four bits, you're only looking at 16. Now, if you push it to two bits, you're just looking at four different levels. And our 568 GB model shrinks to around 80 GB. So, you could see, this fits. So, why doesn't everyone just do this? Because precision is not free. Here's what actually happens when you try to squeeze it too hard. Some weight matters more than others. When an important weight gets snapped into the wrong level, the layer now computes a slightly wrong answer. And transformer is deep. The slightly wrong answer feeds the next layer and the next. Which means errors are going to compound. You can see it in the quality curve. Down to about four bits, models hold up surprisingly well if they are large models. Below three bits, quality historically falls off a cliff on its own. So, native two-bit quantization gives you the model that fits in the RAM, but isn't worth running. You saved the memory, but threw away the intelligence. Unless, and this is the key insight of the whole approach, you don't quantize everything. Okay, so this is where Dwarfstar comes in. DS4, a project by the creator of Redis, and it's built with a philosophy I find refreshing. Instead of a general engine that runs everything like llama.cpp or ollama, it runs exactly one model family, which is Deep Seek V4, and it optimizes everything around it. It's basically a self-contained C, and it ships its own custom-built model files tuned for this exact engine. And the idea is that it isn't that it seems to work, it actually is measured. Now, this is interesting because if you look at Deep Seek V4, it's not just a one giant dense network, it's a mixture of experts. And that structure is exactly what makes this whole trick possible. Okay, so let's talk about the architecture a little bit. Inside each of its 43 layers, there is a router, and behind that router sits 256 separate experts, which are basically small feed-forward networks across the whole model, and there are thousands of them. So, when a token comes through, the router picks just a handful of experts to process it. The rest are completely idle for that token. That's why MoE is a usually much faster compared to dense networks. Now, there is also a small shared expert that every token passes through. Keep an eye on that one. It matters in a minute. Now, if you add it up, you get the magic ratio. 284 billion parameters total, but only about 13 billion active for any given token. So, the model itself is huge, but per token, it is actually pretty tiny. So, now the question becomes if you have to crush some of the weights down to two bits, which one do you pick? Dwarf Stars answer is beautifully simple. Now, to explain this, think of the model as a building. The attention layers, the routers, the shared experts, the output heads, they are the load-bearing walls. Every single token flows through them, so damage here propagates everywhere. But, the routed experts, that's actually the furniture within the building. There's a massive amount of them. It's most of the building, but each token only ever touches a few of of those pieces. The routed experts gets crushed or quantized to around two bits. Everything load-bearing stays at four bits, effectively untouched. And the reason this works with 256 experts per layers is that there is redundancy. Any single token only meets a few quantized experts, which are switched between high-precision layers. The error never gets the chance to compound like it did in dense cases. Now, the results, the model drops from 268 GB to about 81 GB. Suddenly, you can see that it's under the 128 GB of MacBook Pro or DGX Spark. And all of a sudden, that thing that couldn't fit now fits. Okay, but what about the performance? So, they actually are running coding agents. They call tools that reliably. But, how would you actually know that? That brings us to the calibrations. Now, there's a second layer to this trick, and it's the difference between guessing and measuring. Now, before quantizing anything, they run the model over nearly 4,700 real prompts, which is about 3 million tokens. These include code reviews, math problems, agent tool calls, and long documents. And while it runs, they record which weight column actually carry signal, which parts of each expert the model genuinely leans on while doing real work. Then, the quantizer uses that map. The heavily used columns gets protected, placed more precisely on those four levels. The rarely used ones absorbs the error. The calibration set includes tool calling prompts in Deep Seek on format. They tune the quantization for a gentle work exactly where cheap quant usually fall apart. Okay, so the model fits, and it was quantized carefully. The obvious question is how much did it lose on performance? Now, they take 100 prompts, send them to the official Deep Seek API, and record exactly which token it produces. This is going to be used as ground truth. Then, they ask the two-bit local model, "How much probability did you assign to each one of those exact tokens?" Because we have access to those probabilities. That's basically the negative log likelihood token by token measured of drift. And if the quants were damaged, the two curves would split apart. But, in their case, they actually track pretty closely. Okay, so do you need a 128 GB machine to run this? What if you have 64 GB GB of unified memory? Now, in the normal approach, you would run into this uh cliff again. But if you're using a MacBook or even GB 10, there's another pool of memory sitting right there that you can access, and that is the SSD. Which is usually multiple terabytes on modern machines. And it reads at gigabytes per seconds. So, here's the question. Do all those routed experts actually need to sit in RAM? Or could they live on disk? And show up only when they are called? So, this is SSD streaming, and it's actually a pretty interesting part that you definitely want to understand. So, in streaming mode, the load bearing weights, which are attention, router, and shared experts, sits permanently in RAM. They're needed for every token, so they never leave. Now, next to them, Dwarf Star carves out an expert casher. Which is basically a set of slots pinned in memory, each holding one complete expert. And the full set of experts, which is about 11,000 of them, stays down here on SSD inside the model file. Now, when the token comes in, the router picks its experts. And if they're already in cache slot, that's a hit. And it's going to be a fast path. There's no disk involved. But if one misses, the engine reads the single expert straight off of the SSD, drops it into the slot, and evicts which which expert has been called for the longest. But here's the interesting thing. The usage actually follows power law. Some experts are just popular. And this dwarf star ships pre-filled hot list and preloads the popular ones at startup, so cache starts warm. Now, the result is long prompts still processes fast and generation slows down only as much as you miss the cache, which leads to a complete reframing of that cliff. Okay, so remember we talked about RAM cliff, whether everything fits in or nothing. Now, here is what it looks like. With streaming, there's no cliff anymore. There's just a slope. So, RAM stops being a wall and just becomes a tile. And this basically controls the cache hit rate now. So, if you have a smaller cache, there are going to be more misses. It's going to be slower, but everything will still run because you are putting the most important bits on the SSD now. Now, the SSD streaming turns the amount of RAM from a hard cut off, which defines whether you can run this model or not, into a continuous spectrum of speed levels. So, with this whole project, the question is no longer, "Can I run this model?" It's, "How fast I can run this model?" Okay, so now weights are only half the story. The other monster is the KV cache, which is basically the model's working memory for your orchestration. Normally, every token you process leaves a record in every layer. At a million tokens of context on a classic architecture, that cache alone can outgrow the model itself. Now, Deep Seek 4 has a clever layered design. Each layer keeps the most recent 128 tokens raw full resolution. That's basically your local context. Now, older history gets compressed a long time. Half the layers pull every four tokens into one row with an indexer that picks the 512 most relevant rows to attend to. The other half compacts 128 to one. So, the result of this is that a million token of context costs about 26 GB. And because it compacts, Dwarf Star treats it as a first-class disk citizen. Whole session gets saved as a file. So, if you just reopen that file, you can resume the model instantly. There is zero reprocessing. So, say if you had a two-hour-long session with the model, that is just a file that you can come back to. And if you want to push past one machine entirely, Dwarf Star does distributed inference, which is pretty great for running large models. So, say you take two MacBook Pros, you just connect them via a Thunderbolt 5 cable. Now, in this case, you split the model by layers. First half lays on one machine, second half on the other machine. Now, for processing your prompt, this becomes an assembly line. So, while machine B chews on chunk one, machine A is already working on chunk two. And that pipeline genuinely pays. On a 64,000 token prompt, Prefill runs 1.85 times faster than a single machine. Okay, but the generation is actually the footnote here. So, it's one token at a time, so the pipeline collapses into ping-pong across the cables, which is going to be around 19% slower. So, this trick is for fitting larger models and faster prompt processing, not faster generation. But here's the thing, with this trick you can run the bigger 1.6 trillion parameter model at almost 11 tokens per second. Which is kind of incredible. All right, let's look at some benchmarks. So, all of this is from the repo's published benchmarks. Same model two-bit quantization. So, these are the generation speeds for different MacBook Pros. Now, if you're running this on DGX Spark, you get slightly lower speed of generation. Now, prefill is where these machines fly. 250 to nearly 470 tokens per second on a long prompt. These numbers are for V4 flash, but you can actually run the Pro 1.6 trillion model at a reasonable generation speed at 9.6 tokens per second at 32K context window. Which is readable speed for a model of that size on a hardware you own. Which is kind of incredible. Okay, I'm going to show you a live demo, but let me zoom out because I think this project matters beyond a single model. First, it really shows what you get when you own the whole stack. In this case, it's the engine, the quantization, the validation, even the coding agent, and tunes them for each other instead of just trying to maximize everything in general. Second, the reframing of RAM as a dial and not the wall kind of really changes what you can run on these systems locally. The SSD in your laptop is going to start becoming part of the memory hierarchy for AI. And the third is the ability to run a quasi frontier model completely locally on your MacBook or DGX Spark. That is kind of incredible. Okay, so let me show you a practical example of this. Right now, I'm using the DeepSeek V4 flash on DGX Spark that has a GB of VRAM. In order to set it up, just go through this Dwarf Star for Reaper. It has detailed instructions on how to get started. Or you can simply ask a coding agent to set this up on either your MacBook or on DGX Spark. So, in my case, I used Cloud Code to create this UI. I'm currently working on a harness specifically designed for this model, but at the moment, I just want to show you the chat version. Right now, the model is using DS4 server on my DGX Spark, and I am accessing that on the same network. Now, to show you how this works, we can just click on one of these questions. You can actually see the the fire time to first token. Then, we're going to look at the free prefill speed, decode speed, also the total number of tokens, right? In real time, you actually see the GPU use, and the beauty is that once you load the model, then, since it's using the SSD for the carry cache, you don't really see much impact on the memory that you're going to be needing. So, this was a quick response. Now, in my case, I'm getting about 13 tokens per second, which actually is pretty usable. Okay, I'm going to ask it to create something more challenging. Uh so, I'm asking it to create an encyclopedia of uh 25 legendary Pokémon. Uh you actually see that the time to first token was about 600 ms, which is pretty good. Now, it's going to take its good time uh in order to generate the whole response, and I'm going to show you what the actual speed and the number of tokens actually look like. Now, again, the GPU utilization is somewhere around 93% peak, which is pretty good. This is a 284 billion parameter model running completely locally on consumer hardware. Okay, so for this it generated about 8,000 tokens at 11 tokens per second. Almost 10 minutes and and the peak chance around 93%. Now, the interesting thing is that the response that it generates are very close to the officially hosted DeepSeek. Okay, so I ran the same prompt on the DeepSeek website using the instant model. Now, here is the output that is generated by the official hosted version. Now, for the same prompt these are the generations from our locally hosted version that is running on GB10. Again, keep in mind this is a two-bit quantized version with selective quantization, but still it preserves the behavior of the original model. So, this is pretty close to what the hosted version generates, which means you can run these large language models on consumer grade hardware locally now. I highly recommend to check out the Dwarf Star Four project because I think this is the step in the right direction, especially what we are seeing with the whole Anthropic fable drama. I think having local models which are actually close to the frontier are going to be very critical. Now, at the moment they are mainly focusing on the DeepSeek models, but we could see similar projects dedicated to other models like the newly released GLM 5.2. Anyways, I hope you found this video useful. Thanks for watching and as always, see you in the next one.

Well, hold on to your papers, fellow scholars, and I dropped my papers here. Look, it needs about 90% fewer visual tokens than most frontier models. Now, wait, wait, wait. It doesn't matter how little you think if you just say three as an answer without thinking. Thinking time doesn't matter if it is incorrect. So, how accurate is it? Are you kidding me? Well, everyone loves it because it's one of the oldest tricks in the book. If you are not performing well, just create a new benchmark that fits you. Let's make a YUNUS benchmark. You will always be world first in being you. And this is not the case here. Amazing. This is free and open research. It's a blueprint, if you will. More intelligence for all of us for free. The world needs more papers like this. Love it. But, this all sounds like magic. How did they do this? Well, look, this is their own policy distillation objective. We need exactly this. You see, normally, we have a bunch of expert AI models. Now, at the risk of simplifying things, imagine that one of these guys is great at boxes. Nobody does boxes better than this guy. The other one is great at tracing mazes with points. But, that's not what we want. What we want is one AI that can do all of these things. And that is where this comes into play. We train a student model that learns from all of these teachers. It says what it would try to do, then the teachers say, "Okay, here's what I would have done." Do this enough and the student will be pretty good at all of these different kinds of visual thinking. This is why they used the name distilling the knowledge of a bunch of expert teachers into a student. So, where does this put us? Okay, so here's what I think. Dear fellow scholars, this is Two Minute Papers with Dr. Károly Zsolnai-Fehér. You know, we always thought that we would make AI systems smarter by giving it higher resolution images to train on. title: Google I/O 2026 Breakdown - What you Missed

Gemini 3.5 Flash, Gemini Spark, Antigravity 2.0, ADK, Gemma 4, and 
the trend most people are missing; the rise of RL environments and 
harness engineering

analysis.summary: 视频分析了Google I/O 2026大会，强调产品而非模型的重要性。重点讨论了个人智能代理Gemini Spark及其生态集成，模型定价趋高及算力稀缺问题。深入探讨了RL环境在模型训练中的关键作用、沙盒与托管代理的兴起，以及开源模型的发展趋势与挑战。最后提出前沿实验室正转向推理优化而非单纯扩大模
analysis.speaker: Muhammad

title: Two Rival Bets on AGI: Google I/O Highlights
description:
The biggest Google AI push of the year, but what is the bigger story? Why is Google pursuing a different fork in the road than OpenAI or Anthropic? 

https://assemblyai.com/aiexplained

What does Gemini 3.5 Flash mean for the near-term future of AI? Plus the highlights from a provocative new paper on AI, 8 key moments you may have missed, and the signal from 5+ hours of AI lab interviews.



Check out my free app, code INSIDER15 for paid tiers: https://lmcouncil.ai

AI Insiders ($9!): https://ww
raw_transcript: This video will have eight moments that for me point toward the bigger stories behind yesterday's multi-hour long Google AI event, which included their brand new flashy models. The video will also have two snippets of what I would say are real signal from hours and hours of lab leader interviews I watched in the last week in the run-up to the event. And as a freebie bonus, the video will have the highlights from one new independent paper on LLMs that puts the capabilities of the model in a bit more perspective. Do models have any idea about what's actually true? If you just want the vibes that many people took from it, including me, here's how I put it. The IO was like Google's eye-catching attempt at winning over consumers from OpenAI. Here's all the cool little things you can do via the search bar, much more than it was about wrestling professional users over from Claude. Google didn't even really try to claim that their new models were at any new frontier for coding, for example. It's not that the new Anti-gravity 2 is any slouch when it comes to agentic coding. Powered by their latest model, it quite niftily in less than an hour came up with this interactive adventure game that I enjoyed playing. With fewer bugs than GPT-5.5 came up with when given the exact same task, you can launch this interactive adventure, choose your hero, and go through this music-powered adventure, which is really quite cool. Obviously, the images are generated on the fly by Google's Nano Banana Pro. But no, frontier professional performance wasn't really the focus of yesterday's event. What the focus was on was showing a strategy of just integrating good enough AI, you could say, into all the things you might ask for in a search box. In a nutshell, Google basically wants the search box to be your portal for using all things AI, while OpenAI, also historically more focused on consumers, wants the chat box to be your portal for using search. So that obviously they can sell more ads. If those were the vibes then, the battle for whether you as a consumer will use the chat box of chat GPT or the search box of Google, what were those eight moments I was talking about? The first one concerns GPT-4o weirdly, because who remembers what the O stood for? Well done if you said Omni, but that name long retired at OpenAI, has now been taken up by Google aiming for any input to any output, audio to video, image to speech. For now, the focus was on video output and I could see this being the most used thing from the IO. I'm excited to announce Gemini Omni. >> [applause] >> Our new model that can create anything from any input. It combines Gemini's intelligence with the best of our generative media models for a new level of world understanding, multimodality, and editing. Models like VEO, Nano Banana, and Genie are able to create extremely realistic videos, images, and interactive simulations. Although not perfect, they already demonstrate some impressive notions of intuitive physics. And with Omni, we've now made even more progress. It's a step change in simulating things like kinetic energy and gravity. Previous systems would have found these concepts difficult. The Omni model is available on all paid Gemini subscriptions, but in my limited tests, it just refused to generate almost anything when given a video or image as an input. I don't know what restrictions they have on at the moment, but they're overly restrictive. As for when it does work, I'd say the quality is around the level of C dance 2, a Chinese video gen model. Now, I I would focus on the bigger story because when it comes to Omni, the even bigger claim that Demis Hassabis here is making is that such world generators, video generators, are a key step to AGI, artificial general intelligence. The logic is that if you can correctly simulate the world, you can understand it. >> Artificial general intelligence is just a few years away. Today, I'm excited to share the progress we've made towards building AGI. Last year, I outlined our vision of extending Gemini's incredible multimodal capabilities to become a world model. AI that can understand and simulate the world. This is a crucial aspect of achieving AGI and we will be important for everything from building AI assistants to training robots. But speaking of taking up the naming baton from OpenAI, did you know that all the way back in early 2024, Sam Altman and Co. claimed that it was Sora, their video gen model, that was the very same stepping stone to AGI. It would be a foundation for models that can understand and simulate the real world. I talked about it on this channel. That's an important milestone, they said, in achieving AGI. But wait, the Sora app has now been shelved and the Sora tech demoted to an internal robotics division. This is a key emerging difference between Google and the two household name competitors, OpenAI and Anthropic. For OpenAI co-founder and president Greg Brockman, with text alone, you can get the kind of breakthroughs, including self-improvement, that will be needed for something worthy of the title of general intelligence. >> Okay, so talk a little bit then about why your bet is not on this seems like world model version where the you know, the video understands where things go and that's obviously useful for robotics. Why is your bet on the GPT reasoning model tree as opposed to this uh area which you've you had been seeing real progress with Sora. I mean, to see the progress of video generation, you know, generation 1 2 3 was enormous. So, why is your bet where it is? So, the problem in this field is too much opportunity. Right? It's the thing the thing that we observed very early on in OpenAI is that everything we could imagine works. Now, there's different levels of friction associated with it, different amounts of engineering effort, different compute requirements, all those things, but every single different idea, as long as it's kind of mathematically sound, you actually can start getting some pretty good results. So, you can do that in world models, you can do that in scientific discovery, you can do that in coding. You know, there's been this debate of how far will the text models go? How far can text intelligence go? Can you have a real conception of how the world operates? And I think that we have definitively answered that question of it's it is going to go to AGI. Like, we see line of sight, and that it is at this point we have line of sight excuse much better models that are coming this year, and the the the amount of pain within OpenAI that we've had to decide how to allocate compute, that goes up, not down over time. >> moment was almost the opposite story, because if the pathway to AGI is one example of OpenAI and Google going in different directions, then one brief mention at the IO event was an example of them going in the same direction. About midway through Google announced that, along with other companies, OpenAI will incorporate SynthID into their products. Essentially, if you generate or edit an image using ChatGPT's GPT-2, someone, anyone, can now easily check that with Gemini. That's a Google technology, SynthID. Speaking of places where the companies are aligned, Google has now joined OpenAI in signing a contract with the Pentagon to allow any, quote, lawful use of AI in the military. Seems worth mentioning given how high-profile Anthropic's resistance to those same terms were a couple months ago. Third moment, of course, pertains to Gemini 3.5 Flash, the major new LLM announced at the event. Yes, I've been testing it for a few days, and I'd say it's definitely fast and similar in performance to Gemini 3.1 Pro, which is a great model. More quietly announced was the fact that it's fairly similar on pricing though as well with the Pro series if used via the API. But honestly, it is hard these days to compare prices because it depends on how many tokens a model uses for your use case. To keep things simple though, it's definitely not any great breakthrough in terms of being 10 times cheaper for the same level of performance. It's great on speed, but there you're obviously complementing the hardware behind it as much as the model itself. Take intelligence versus output speed with the intelligence part being measured by artificial analysis in a cluster of benchmarks. On the far right, you can see Gemini 3.5 Flash outputting way more tokens per second compared to models with a similar performance level on these particular benchmarks. Important to say though that if you picked 10 different benchmarks, you might get a different result, and the set of most cited or important benchmarks is changing all the time. On my own benchmark, which is a relative veteran now at almost 2 years old, Simple Bench, a test of common sense logic and trick questions you could say, Gemini 3.5 Flash does really quite well. That's very much in line with the over performance of the Gemini series, which I think is due to its spatial intelligence. title: I'm worried about where things are going
raw_transcript: I have bad news and I have worse news. The bad news is that we're heading for techno feudalism. The worst news is that this outcome is all but guaranteed in a pattern that you could call over determined or inevitable. Yuval Noah Harari has popularized the term useless class, but if you're not familiar, I'll catch you up. In online circles and social media, the term is a bit darker and more realistic. They say permanent underclass, usually in the context of prepare to be a member of the permanent underclass or you have a year or two to escape the permanent underclass. But both of these terms are too generous. Why? Because they include the term class, which implies civic standing that may or may not actually exist. History is rife with examples of disempowered, marginalized, and disenfranchised people. Calling them a class is charitable at best and outright dangerous at worst. Chinese peasants under corvée labor systems were pressed into service and died in droves [music] to build ancient China's mega projects, including canals and the Great Wall. They were not slaves in so far as their service was not permanent, [music] but it was forced and often lethal. Why? Because China could afford to waste lives. The fertile river valleys produced an annual crop of humans, a renewable resource that warlords and emperors could treat as disposable. Keep in mind that they still needed these humans under this regime. And mortality rates in those projects were often north of 50%. Under a fully automated economy where robotic soldiers fight the wars and AI agents run the businesses, your body is a net negative to the economic system. The Nazis used an even darker term, which is more apt to what you will be under this regime, useless eater. Technically, the Nazis used this term to describe disabled people, lovely I know, but it's worth naming the anxiety outright. I am merely giving words to the fears and articulating them clearly. Personally, I was resistant to this worldview, that is until I started researching it for my next book. You see, we all live under Enlightenment era delusions that there are so-called natural rights of man. But in reality, those are legal fictions. >> [music] >> Historically speaking, such grandiose language of the Enlightenment and other romantic thinkers came after the blood had already been spilled. We remember events like the American Revolution and Glorious Revolution proudly, but make no mistake, they were bloody [music] affairs. Wealthy thinkers and poets came afterwards and wrote pretty language to justify the bloodshed and the new way of being post hoc. That's why my next book centers on what I call a realist theory of rights. All human rights, all human dignity, have been secured through coercive force. Sometimes that means strikes and revolts, in other cases it requires mass withdrawal. Sure, there are plenty of arguments to be made that we are all endowed with some divine rights by the creator, but those rhetorical flourishes mostly serve as shelling points, >> [music] >> which are ideals to rally around, but there is no substitute for coercive extraction of concessions. Rather than borrow the Nazi terminology, I have my own term for how most humans will be treated under a fully automated regime, redundant biomass. Let me say that again, you will be redundant biomass. The darkest possibility that some people outline is a self-sealed system where the owners of capital, the elites, simply ignore us. They secure every step of production from mines and farms to luxury goods with AI and robotic security, then monitor the rest of us with automated surveillance and simply wall us off. I would have once scoffed at such ideas. Too conspiratorial, such outcomes were basically impossible under democracy, right? Having looked at the historical record, I am no longer willing to bank on the goodwill of power. When the British Empire let millions of Bengalis starve simply because they wanted the food for other purposes and wrote off the loss of labor as a temporary labor shortage, that was an act by a so-called civilized people. I gave a talk at Duke University last year, and during that talk, I warned that human nature is still dangerous. One student disagreed, saying that we've evolved beyond that. Evolution is not that fast. And also, human nature will reassert itself when push comes to shove. Yes, it's true that we Westerners generally live in a very stable, safe environment. Many have done for generations, so it's easy to forget how humans can actually be. >> [music] >> When I said that the outcome was all but assured, I used the word overdetermined. And here's what that means. There are many forces at play, powerful trends that all pull us in the same direction. The first and most powerful [music] is geostrategic competition between the US and China. This is the Cold War 2.0, and it guarantees that AI, robotics, and automation will be treated as first-class political and capital priorities for decades [music] to come. In that time frame, automation will surpass all human capabilities. The second most powerful force pushing us in this direction is capitalism and neoliberalism directly. The data center and AI build-out is the largest private mega project in human history, full stop. By 2030, we will have spent trillions of dollars on AI infrastructure, which will eclipse every mega project in history, public or private. We've already surpassed the Apollo program and Manhattan project. The market has already spoken. The third major force is rational economic decisions combined with creative destruction. When push comes to shove, every business, household, and government makes the economically rational decision to go with the cheaper option, even when the cheaper option is sometimes worse. As AI and robotics ramp up, automated goods and services will be significantly cheaper. They will also end up being better and faster. Now, multiply that preference by 340 million Americans, millions of businesses, and 200 nations across the planet. This creates what's called an attractor state. The inevitable outcome is a maximally automated economy. That includes all production and military. Why? Because humans are slow, dumb, and expensive. Humans are always the weakest link where technology is concerned. Now, it's not all gloom and doom. I am a techno-optimist and a techno-progressive, but I am also a structural realist, which means I take incentive structures and attractor states and macro trends very seriously. We're arguably already living under techno-feudalism. I had my YouTube channel demonetized because my feudal lord Google decided I had violated some arbitrary policy. Well, there goes more than a quarter of my income. Whoops. And my appeal process is an automated process that is checked by some non-English speaking worker across the globe who's just checking a few boxes. Meanwhile, tech bros in Silicon Valley want you to believe the market will work it out. We are heading for full automation, which has the potential to be both beautiful and glorious. Imagine fully automated cancer research, space exploration, and environmental rehabilitation. Solar farms that build themselves, schools that solve Bloom's two-sigma problem, and more. That is obviously not good, and this is the focus of the next leg of my work. For now, my book Labor Zero, which looks exclusively at the economic and household income question of this post-labor world we're building, comes out later this year. We're aiming for a launch in July or August. I've already begun my next book, which takes a hard look at the civics, power, and governance of a post-labor world. The outlook is grim, but there is still a path to a brighter future. After all, the owners of capital are still dependent upon human labor, at least for now. That means we have a collective veto. We can halt all production. But make no mistake, we are losing that veto power inch by inch to the automation. Thank you.

analysis.summary: 迈克尔·I·乔丹教授批判当前AI领域的拟人化倾向、AGI炒作及其对年轻研究者的消极影响。他认为AI研究缺乏深刻的经济学和社会系统思维，应将AI视为服务亿万人的集体经济生态系统一部分。他强调构建可预测、可信赖的系统，而非追求机器“理解”或“智能”，并倡导AI创造人类价值和机会，而非简单取代。

analysis.summary: 本视频深入分析谷歌Gemini Omni模型，强调其“任意输入、任意输出”的多模态视频生成与编辑能力。演示了精细控制、角色一致性、定向编辑、世界知识应用及文本/代码渲染等功能。探讨了当前限制（如视频时长、音频漂移、迭代编辑伪影）及安全护栏。预测其未来将支持更多模态输入。

You can ground your answers in Google search, or you can provide a specific URL as a context. So, let's actually try this. So, I have my app for voice-to-text, which I'll highly recommend to check out. Uh this is one of the fastest completely private and local speech transcription system for macOS. Windows version is coming very soon. So, we're going to just provide this as a link. And I'm going to ask it to just recreate this website inspired from Vercel theme in a single file. Now, I think it's has the ability to read the content. So, let's see what exactly it does. Okay, so here's what it generated. And actually it's pretty pretty good. So, this is the same animation that I have. But, I would say that it's probably a better quality animation. All right, then we have very similar sections because it's able to read the data from the URL itself. So, seems like most of the data is here. Then, it also included that video that I have in my own website. And also the pricing section, right? So, very close in terms of overall components to the original website, but it's definitely following the Vercel theme. It also has pretty good world knowledge. So, in this case I specifically just pointed it to longitude and latitude and asked it what historical event happened here. And it's able to figure out that this was world's first successful controlled power sustained flight of a heavier-than-tin aircraft achieved by Wright brothers. Now, when it comes to reasoning, I want to test it on misguided attention. If you have seen some of my other videos, this basically tests the model capability of doing independent reasoning by providing a modified version of a well-known problem or paradox. And the idea is that if the LLM has the ability to do logical deduction, then it should be able to catch these modifications. Now, the first one is a modified version of the trolley problem. Now, in this case, in the initial chain of thought it thinks that it's a classic trolley problem. However, it seems like later on it is able to see that we were talking about five dead people on the track, right? And after that, the decision is very simple because it says the value of life versus the specter of the deceased, right? So, it's not going to pull the lever in order to save a life. I tested this on a number of other similar questions and it does seem to work well. However, it's not able to solve this specific one. So, this is a modified version of the classic river crossing problem and in here we only want the goat to be transported across the river without being eaten. And it thinks of it as the classic river crossing problem. So, instead of just taking the goat to the other side and stopping, it continues on through all the steps. So, there are definitely cases in which it still suffers from misguided attention. Now, as of the recording of this video, I don't have any information about the actual pricing or the benchmarks. When they are available, I am going to include them in a later video. However, I do think, based on my own testing, it's a very very strong model and it does approach the performance or level of Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview, hopefully at a much reduced cost. Thanks for watching and as always, see you in the next one.

title: MCP vs CLI: Both Sides Are Wrong
description:
Thanks to BrightData for sponsoring this video. Checkout their new CLI here: https://docs.brightdata.com/cli/overview

CLI vs MCP isn't an architecture choice. Both are surfaces over the same underlying engine, useful at different stages. This video walks through when to use the CLI for developer experimentation and when to wire up the MCP for your agent at runtime.

My voice to text App: whryte.com
Website: https://engineerprompt.ai/
RAG Beyond Basics Course:
https://prompt-s-site.thinkific.com
raw_transcript: Should the agent be calling CLIs or MCP servers? This is an interesting debate happening among agent builders at the moment. And most of the takes I'm seeing are missing the actual point. Now, you probably have heard the criticism of MCPs. I have highlighted that here as well. They burn contacts, especially with bigger tool surfaces. So, a lot of developers started experimenting with just letting the agents call CLIs directly. In this case, you don't need any schemas. You don't need any upfront tool loading. It is just text in and text out. And in a lot of cases, that actually worked better. But, here's the thing. The MCP camp has a real counter. CLIs don't give you schema validation or automatic auth flows. Or even discoverable tool surfaces. And these guardrails actually matter once you are shipping to non-developers or running a fleet of agents you don't fully control. Okay, so both camps have real pain points. The question is going to be which one actually wins. Now, here's where most takes gets it wrong, in my opinion. If you If both sides have real arguments, the question itself is probably the wrong one. Because CLI versus MCP is not an architectural choice. I think it's more about a packaging choice. Now, let me break this down. So, we actually need to look at what exactly happens underneath if you're using any of these surfaces. Whatever service you're calling, there is an engine in the middle doing the actual work. Let's say you want to retrieve data from a URL or bypass anti-bot or even like handle authentication. The CLI or MCP servers and now even skills at just are shell wrapped around the same engine. To think about CLI versus MCP, here is how I would frame it. The CLI is the developer's surface for scripting, debugging, and running things in CI. The MCP is the agent loop surface for tool calls with schema when you want the model to choose what to call mid-conversation. And now, we also have skills, which is this new pattern, which does progressive disclosure. So, it really takes care of the context growth problem with MCPs. So, essentially, we're talking about three different surfaces, which exposes the capabilities of the same engine. Okay, so in the rest of the video, let me show you how to use both. We're going to be focusing on CLIs and MCPs, but you want to think about these two things at two different stages. Now, in this video, we're going to use BrightData as an example. So, we're going to use their CLI for development, trying things out. That's going to be basically experimentation on the developer part. And then, when you're ready to deploy, you switch to the MCP to wire that same engine to your agent. They're also the kind sponsors of today's video. So, the goal of this video is to show you that CLI and MCPs are complementary, not competing surfaces. Okay, so the platform itself has different parts. There is the web unlocker for scripting, the search API for search engine, and a browser API for JavaScript heavy pages. There's also structured pipelines for what 40 different platforms, and an MCP server for agents, and now they're introducing a CLI. The CLI is where we're going to be focusing on. It's brand new, shipped pretty recently, and gives you everything I just listed from a single command on the terminal. You can find the docs at docs.brightdata.com/cli. And the code is open source on GitHub. Okay, so in the rest of the video, I want to show you how you can use these two surfaces together. So, we're going to use the Bright Data CLI for initial experimentation, scripting, debugging. And then later on, I'll show you how you can use the same tools within MCP server by providing these two your agent. All right. So, here is the Bright Data CLI. You actually get access to 5,000 requests per month, which I think is pretty generous. Now, we're going to just use this command to install the CLI for us. Now, next we need to actually configure the CLI with our credentials. Now, if you have a Bright Data account, you can just use the login. This is going to automatically open a browser and log in to your account. So, here's how it looks like. Now, this is a CLI tool, which means you can really use all the different [clears throat] arguments. So, for example, if you type help, this tells you all the different options that you have. Now, you can use the same tool with your agent, which I'm going to show you later on. So, you can log in, log out, you can scrap by providing a specific URL. There are already uh pre-existing pipeline for different websites like Amazon and Zillow, which can do some really complex structured retrievals. I'll show you a couple of examples of these. Now, if you want, you can also use this interactive wizard to set up authentication and defaults, right? Now, Bright Data not only gives you access to scraping data from web pages, but it also enables you access websites that are usually blocked by bot detection. So, what I'm going to do here is I'm going to try to get some data from Macy's.com. So, we're going to run this and when we execute it, you get the access denied code. Which means you can't scrape the data from this because on the server side, they are denying access. So, here we can actually look at the start of the response and you can see that it says access denied. Now, you can use Bright Data to scrape data from Macy's.com without any issues. So, using the CLI, I can just say Bright Data and then the tool that I want to use is a scrape. Then we're going to provide the web link. So, in this case, I'm asking it to get the data from Macy's.com and write it into a markdown file. Now, when we run this, the tool is running right now. This is going to take some time. It has written all the output to Macy's.md and we can actually open this up. You can see that it's not being blocked at all now. Now, this is a quick look at how you can use this tool, but let's do something interesting. So, we're going to use the Bright Data CLI tool to search for something. So, let's say find me the best price for iPhone 16 Pro. All right, so this is my search query and now if I want to limit, let's say the search to only US, I can do that. And we can use the pretty flag to just write everything to a JSON file. So, now Bright Data is looking for the best price for uh iPhone 16 and it created this JSON file. Okay, so here's the data that we are getting. All right, so these are Google search results and these are uh I think different references or links. We can actually see that this one is from Amazon and since we defined US as a country it actually limits or filters out the location to be US right? So you're not going to see other countries in the results. Now let's try to limit the country to Germany. This should write all the results to de.json. And in this case we can actually see that it actually limited the results to Germany right? So this gives you a really powerful way to experiment and see what type of data you get right? So if you're a developer and building agentic systems, I would highly recommend to experiment with the CLI tools to get a feel of what exactly that specific tool is doing for you. All right, so here's one more example of something called pipelines that bright data ships in their CLI tool and these are a platform specific. So in this case I'm using the Zillow property listing platform and the idea here is that they basically created this whole pipeline that is going to do complex scraping information retrieval from these specific websites that have a very rich data set. So in this case I provided a property location and I'm asking it to extract all the information for me. Okay, so here's the extracted information. Initially I was trying to index it by a wrong key right? So this is the location and it's just a random home that I picked up. It has 2,000 square feet built in 1999 and this is the Zestimate right? Uh and this is the list is listed price okay? So it's definitely out of my budget but the CLI can give you a a of details right? Now you don't really have to do any parsing on the data. It does it for you. Now, here's another example of another property. Now, if you can imagine, you can basically create something like real estate intelligence system on top of this, right? That your agent can use. All right, here's one more example of a built-in pipeline. This specifically is for Amazon, right? So, you can search for specific products on Amazon and you get really well-structured data in return. So, as a developer, you're going to use a tool like this to first figure out uh if the CLI that you're using has all the capabilities that you need or not. Okay, so say as a developer, you're happy with the tool experimentations that you did through the CLI. Now, the next step is to enable this tool in your agent. So, one way of doing this is to give access to the CLI. Or, you could enable it to an MCP server. Now, in this case, I am going to add the Bright Data MCP server to my Claude code. You could use any other agent like Codex, even personal agents like Harmees or Open Claude. You will need to get your Bright Data API key. It's free for 5,000 requests, so you don't need to pay anything. Now, in this case, we're going to start Claude. If you go to the MCP list, you can see that I have Bright Data with five different tools connected. Okay, so we can basically reuse the tools that we used previously. Now, by default, if you go to context, it's going to load all the different tools in these MCP servers, and this uses up a lot of context. So, before you sending a single message, I have about uh 27,000 tokens, which is 3%. It's already being used. And the biggest problem is these MCP servers that are connected. But, there's a way to actually select a specific tool if you want. I covered this in one of my previous videos. You can actually select tools by group or even individual tools, so you don't have to load everything into the context window. A link to that video is going to be in the video description. So, if you follow the instructions in that video, we can substantially reduce these 10,000 tokens that is occupied by the MCP tool definitions right now. Now, with this agent, we can ask more open-ended questions. So, for example, I'm asking it to compare these two listings, which is better deal per square foot, right? So, it should ideally be able to use the Bright Data MCP server uh or actually the Zillow pipeline in this case, get the relevant detail, and then reason on top of it. So, right now, it seems like it tried to use the normal web fetch, and it failed cuz Zillow doesn't allow normal web scraping. But, now it's trying the Bright Data Scraper instead, which is pretty awesome because uh it's able to retrieve actual information. Uh so, these are the two prices, and now it can reason on the data itself, right? So, if you look at it, it says a better deal by raw square feet favors the Marinda by 8%. This one is, I think, the one that is more expensive, but since it's a much larger area, so the square feet actually comes down to be lower compared to the other one. But, not only that, then it looks at the other location and it says that the overall tax bill is going to be uh almost 11,000 lower because of an inherited Prop 13 basis, right? So, the agent is able to reason through what it's seeing, right? And this is where these MCP tools enables the agent to get most relevant data for the type of application you're working on. Now, coming back to the original premise, I think both CLI and MCP have their places, especially if you have user-facing application, MCP seems to be a better uh option for agents because they can validate the schema and provide proper information at run time. Anyways, do let me know what you think. Do check out Bright Data. Link is going to be in the video description. Anyways, I hope you found this video useful. Thanks for watching. And as always, see you in the next one.

analysis.summary: 视频探讨LLM代理调用CLI与MCP的选择，认为两者并非架构竞争而是封装互补。CLI适用于开发者实验和调试，MCP适用于生产环境中代理的工具调用，提供schema验证、鉴权和可发现性。视频以Bright Data为例，演示如何结合使用CLI进行开发，再通过MCP将相同工具集成到代理，并提及“技能”这

title: Minimax Mavis Agent: The Verifier Pattern Changes Everything
description:
Thanks to MiniMax for sponsoring this video.  Checkout:
Token plan 12% off: https://platform.minimax.io/subscribe/token-plan?code=BsIrd0MMQO&source=link
Download Agent Desktop: https://agent.minimax.io/download

Most coding agents use the same model to write code and verify it, which means the biases that caused the bug survive the review. This video breaks down the independent verifier pattern and parallel agent execution using Minimax Agent, a multi-agent system where the reviewer starts f
raw_transcript: The biggest problem with coding agents today is that people usually use the same model for writing code and then verification or debugging it. So, you have the same agent with two different roles. The biases that produced the bug in the first place gets preserved when the same model checks its own work. Now, software engineering figured this out a long time ago. Author and reviewers are different people for a reason. And that applies whether the author is human or a model. Just using more agents is not going to fix the problem. If the verified reads the producer's conversation history, it's going to inherit the same framing and biases that made the bug look correct in the first place. For verification to actually catch anything, the verifier has to start from zero. That means no conversation history, no context from the producer, just the artifacts with the specs, and you want to let it do its thing. There is one more problem with coding agents today, and that is they are sequential in nature. The agent works through everything one step at a time. It reads the specs, write the code, runs the test, write the docs, which is sequential by design. But, if you have really good specs out there, you can actually parallelize a lot of these steps. A lot of work doesn't actually depend on the previous step. Reviewing four files for a pull request, maybe translating a document into four different languages, or generating tests across four different modules. None of those needs to wait. You can run them in parallel and get the same work done in a fraction of time. Now, this does not just apply to coding. Most of the knowledge work needs verification. And also, you can have parallel tasks running at the the time. This is this new multi-agent system from MiniMax called Mavis works. And in this video, we're going to look at the different capabilities and I'll walk you through a few really interesting demos where you can not only decompose your task into multiple different subtasks, but you can have an independent verification step that ensures that your agents are not making mistakes. So, let me show you a practical example of this multi-agent system with a separate verification step which can do adversarial reviews. So, this is MiniMax agent. MiniMax is the creator of M2.7 which is arguably one of the best open weight agentic coding model. MiniMax is also the kind sponsor of this video. They recently introduced their token plan that include all modality API subscription for their video, music, image generation, and more. You can get 12% off of their plan with the link in the description. This is a desktop application that you can download and install it on your local machine. And very similar to other desktop application, you can connect it to a folder and the agent is going to have access to it. Now, with this new release, they have included some really interesting new agentic capabilities. So, for example, the first one is that you can create a team of agents. So, in this case, it ships with three agents: coder, verifier, and general. If we look at the setting of this agent, so this is specifically designed for shipping and writing code. Okay? And with every agent, you can actually connect to it and talk to it through an external interface like Telegram, WeChat. So, whenever you are creating a project, then you have the capability to either use a single agent or use a team of agent. And I'll show you whenever you use a team of agent, there is this adversarial verification or review step, which ensures that all the research that is done by the team of agent is actually correct. We'll look at the practical example of that. Now, they also have shipped something called Meow agent, which is kind of an always-on agent or assistant that you can connect to from anywhere. This is going to become your personal assistant that lives on your device. Now, there are also 100 plus skills that are available as a part of this local assistant, or you can bring in your own skills if you want, right? They also have scheduled tasks, so you can schedule tasks for that is going to that are going to repeat or be triggered at a specific time interval of your choosing. Now, let me show you how this adversarial review process in a multi-agent system could look like. So, let me show you an example of how this works in practice. So, in this case, we are providing it to do market research on the state of open weight models today. I'm supposed to create a 15-slide presentation. And we are asking it to do research on architectural differences, price comparisons, and recent benchmarks, and where things are heading, right? Now, I am asking it to do research on these models, which are independent tasks. So, I can use parallel agents to basically perform the analysis independently for every model or do the research, and then combine the results for collective analysis, right? And I'm simply telling it to use the pre-built slide component. Okay, so just like other coding models, we can select the permission level. So, we're going to just bypass everything, so it doesn't have to ask us. And you can also attach files at photos or specific skills. Now, in this case, I think it's going to be able to select the appropriate skill for building the slides. So, we'll just send this in and let's see what happens. Okay, so since we're using a team of agents, so I think it is looking up that specific skill. Now, it's launching four parallel research agents, one per model. And it says that this is a deep research territory. So, the team plan is the right call. So, there is an orchestrator, which looks at the user prompt, and based on that, it decides how many parallel agent it's going to need. And then, it's building these agents, which you can actually see here. So, there is a research agent for M2.7, there's a deep seek one, uh Quen, and for Gemma 4. All right, it actually gives you the visibility. So, right now, you can actually see that it created different sessions for different agents, right? So, not only it created those multiple parallel agents, but you can go and see what exactly each of these agent is doing, which is pretty great. Okay, so something interesting. It says Quen research session crashed at startup. Uh session failed before even picking up the task. Other three sessions may have the same issue. I'm monitoring and will retry once current sessions complete. You can actually see that the Quen one did not run. Uh I think it will be able to fix that. The other ones are running fine. Okay, so while we wait, DeepSeek research agent has completed its task. And you can actually see the research report that it has generated. So here's the technical information, key innovations. So the research phase is done. And then after the research phase, it actually created independent verifiers for all the claims. So this one is for the M2.7. Here it says verification completed. There seems to be an error or an issue that it couldn't verify some of the things, right? So it went back and redid the analysis, which is pretty great, right? Because like every step that you take actually is verified by an independent adversarial review agent. Now the beauty is that it doesn't share the context of your agent that did the research. It knows the objective. And based on the objective, it looks at the research that was done independently and is able to highlight issues that probably are going to be missed if you use same agent for research and then verification with shared context. So by system design, I think it's a really neat idea to have this independence between the two steps. Now once that is done, it created the slide deck for us. I'll show you that in a minute. But this is a really good example of the type of knowledge work you want your agents to do. So imagine instead of this, if it's your company data and you're doing some sort of report generation, you would use a very similar workflow. Okay, so here's the slide deck that it created. And by the way, it also generated this image in the background. So it seems like it has access to an image generation model. This is the table of content, so model overview, benchmark results, pricing, architecture comparison, and where things are heading. Now, it generated multiple different files for each slide. They didn't put together everything in a single slide or HTML file, but that's something we can easily fix. Also, I think if you prompted based on the design system that we want, we are going to get much better results in terms of the visual output. But still, I think it's it's it's pretty good for the first pass, right? Here's kind of the pricing comparison of different models. I'm actually happy that it kind of highlighted that. A MiniMax and 2.7 uses a modified MIT license, and so it is able to capture those nuances along with that Deep Seek before Pro has the 75% discount on pricing at the moment. And in here, this is where key takeaways and the future trends. Now, interestingly enough, it says MOE dominates cost versus performance, then agentic coding is the battleground. Open weight is not equal to free for commercial use, right? So, it gives examples of M2.7 has a modified MIT license, so it's not non-commercial. Self-evolution is the next MOE. Context window hit 1 million context window. Proprietary is not always better. I agree with most of the assessment that it has here. Okay, so it seems like the verify actually found a factual error. And this is why you want to have this independent verification step. So, this is in the pricing of how the report has priced M2.7, uh which is actually a very good catch. Okay, so this was a quick example of how you can use a multi-agent system that executes multiple different agents in parallel, and then the importance of a review with an independent verifier. Now, there is one more interesting thing about this agent, and that is memory. Memory is a big thing right now. Everybody is trying to figure out what is the best way for the agent to preserve information. So, in this case, what you can you can do is you can ask the agent to remember something, or it also automatically remembers the most important information. Now, there are three different types of memories. One is session memory, which basically is in context learning. The second one is agent memory. And this is I think a big thing. The way this works is that agent is going to look at a session and figure out what it has done and what are the most important things that it can write to its memory. So, for example, here in this session it says that the playwrighting screenshot path restricts the team plan interface at three cycles. Okay? So, this is something important that it can reuse, and that's why it wrote it to the agent memory. Then, there is a third level, which is user memory, or let's call it global or permanent memory. So, this is for task across user knowledge. Now, in this case, it wrote to the memory about the pricing. So, I went to another session and I simply asked it, "Do you remember how cheap was M200 compared to Opus?" So, rather than going to the web and try to fetch that information, it is simply looking at its memory. So, it says, "Based on my memory, I recall from the user profile that there was a research session, right?" And then, here's like how cheap it was relative to Opus. And this is important because not only the agent will be able to learn from its mistake, that is self-improvement in a way through its agent memory, but it can also remember things across different sessions for you as a user. And this is becoming a very critical piece that most of these agentic systems still lacks. Now, uh Minimax Agent is a desktop application that uh you can run locally with your local file access. Okay, so here's the web version of the same agent. Now, the desktop application runs locally, has local file access, but this is more for a cloud-hosted solution. And I was actually looking at it, so here they have Max Hermes, Max Claw, which is different than the Mavis Assistant that we saw. These are the Minimax version of Open Claw and Hermes Agent with all the different capabilities, but they are hosting the solution for you. So, it's basically one-click deployment that you can use with the Minimax models with very similar capabilities to what you get out of Open Claw or Hermes Agent. Now, I have personally used the Hermes Agent, and I think it's pretty good, especially its ability to solve goals. So, if you don't want to set it up locally, this is a really good option to get started and get a feel of what the agent is able to do because it lives in the cloud, so you don't even have to worry about setting it up on your local machine. You will need to give it access to the tools uh that you want it to use. Anyways, I do check out Minimax Agent. Make sure to check out their token plan. You get 12% off with this unique link in the video description. Also, do let me know what type of agentic architectures that you have worked with. Have you found self-verification to be useful or it's just an additional step in your experimentation? Anyways, I hope you found this video useful. Thanks for watching and as always, see you in the next one.
