title: The Model Doesn't Matter. The Harness Does. (Cursor + Anthropic)
description:
Get started with SerpApi using 250 free credits: https://serpapi.com/?utm_source=youtube&utm_campaign=promptengineering_may_2026 

I break down what Cursor found about agent harness design and why switching models mid-conversation can reduce performance. I explain how different providers’ models are trained for different edit formats (patch-based vs string replacement), why using the “wrong” tool shape costs extra reasoning and increases mistakes, and how harness quality can make the same mo
raw_transcript: If you're using one of these coding agents that lets you select models from different providers, you're probably using one model for planning and another for execution. However, that probably is not a good idea. Cursor just released this blog post which shows that switching models mid-conversation might actually be hurting the performance of your agent. And coding agents that lets you bring any models may not be giving you the optimal performance for everything. This video, I want to walk you through what they found, why it matters, and what it actually means for the way you pick your tool. Okay, so let me show you what exactly the Cursor team is saying. So, here's a quote. All of our harness abstractions are model agnostic and can be heavily customized for every model we support. For instance, OpenAI's models are trained to edit files using patch-based format, while Anthropic models are trained on string replacement. End quote. And then uh they make the key point. Uh either model could use either tool, but giving it [music] the unfamiliar one costs extra reasoning tokens and produces more mistakes. So, what they are saying here is simple, but the implications are huge. OpenAI's models are trained with patches, kind of like Git diff. Uh Claude models are trained with string replacements, uh which means find this exact text, replace it with the other text. Now, when you give Claude a patch-style tool, you're asking it to operate in a format it does not natively know. And when you give uh GPT models a string replace tool, same problem, but just in reverse. Now, Cursor has the resources to build separate harnesses for every model that they support. They can AB test it. They can tune it for every provider. But, most third-party agents you're using, they probably don't have the resources. There is another problem that every model creators are coming up with their own format. The end result is that most of these harnesses just pick one format and apply it everywhere. And that's why the same model can feel like a genius in one tool and pretty average in another. It's not the model that change, it's the harness around it. Okay, so the customization is just one piece. Cursor's block walk through three other things that you need as a model creator or agent harness creator. Now, the first is dynamic context. Older harnesses loaded everything the model might need up front. But, there is a simple trade-off. If you load too little, the model cannot see what it needs. If you load too much, you're burning tokens that you don't have to burn. Cursor's approach is different. They wrote quote, "We have adopted to increase model's capability by knocking down guardrails and providing more dynamic context, which can be fetched by the agent while it works." End quote. So, the agent can decide what context it needs and when it needs it. The second thing is how they track errors. So, they classify every tool error into three different categories. Invalid arguments, unexpected environments, and provider error. The first two are model mistakes. The third is when the underlying provider actually fails. And here uh are the results in their own words. We're looking at a 10 times reduction for the same model just by the tuning of harness around the model. Most production agents need access to live data from the web. The problem is that web scraping is a pain. You're dealing with CAPTCHAs, rate limits, HTML layouts that change every few weeks, and proxies. All things that have nothing to do with the actual project you're trying to ship. SerpAPI handles all of this for you. You make one API call and you get clean, structured JSON back from Google, Bing, YouTube, and a bunch of others. There's no scraping, no parsing on your end. You just get the data you need. For AI engineering, this fits really well. If you're building an AI agent that needs to look up things, their Google Search API gives you the latest information. Do you need a data set for pre-classified images for training? Their Google Images API gives you the titles, URLs, and thumbnails ready to go. Are you building a research assistant? Their Google Scholar API gives you the full peer-reviewed paper metadata. We're going to uh talk about agents in a few minutes, and a lot of production agent setups use SerpAPI as one of their core tools. You can get started with 250 free credits. Link is going to be in the description or scan the QR code on the screen. Big thanks to SerpAPI for sponsoring this video. And the third is how they measure quality. They call it keep rate. There are two ways. The first is the keep rate of agent-generated code. We track what fraction of those code changes remain in the user's code base after fixed interval of time. So, if you keep the code written by the agents, keep rate goes up. If you as a user delete or rewrite it, keep rate drops. And this is what production grade agent and measurement actually looks like. You're not tracking some vanity benchmarks. The percentage of the agent's work that survive contact with you is what cursor is interested in. Now, harness design is a very hot topic right now. And if you have noticed, I have been creating a lot of videos around it because it's a very interesting engineering problem. Now, cursor is not alone. Anthropic also released a very interesting blog post recently on this exact problem. It's called harness design for long-running application development. The post walks through what they tried first, a single agent. You give it a task and let it work. They call this the solo approach. It ran for 20 minutes. The cost was around $9 and the output barely worked. Then, they built a full harness around the same model. In this case, there were three agents, a planner, a generator, and an evaluator working together. The planner expanded the requests into full product specs. This is basically specs-driven development. The generator worked in sprints, picking up one feature at a time. And the evaluator used the Playwright MCP to actually click through the running app like a real-world user would. Now, this is how you want to divide your projects into multiple different parts, which can be verified. The results in in Tropic's own words, were "The harness was over 20 times more expensive, but the difference in the output quality was immediately apparent." Now, using a complicated harness is going to definitely cost you. But, you're going to get massively better results with the same model but just changing the scaffolding. In a follow-up, run uh they brought it down to 4 hours and $124, which is still expensive. Now, the beauty is that you can actually quantify this. You can run benchmarks uh with different harnesses but same model, and you're going to see dramatically different results. [clears throat] So, one good example of this is SweepBench Pro. Most coding benchmarks let each agent bring its own scaffolding. So, a cursor is one example of that scaffolding, Claude Code is another. SweepBench Pro is different. It forces every model into the same minimal scaffold, uh which is basically a bash-only tool, a fixed turn limit, no custom optimization. So, you can isolate raw model capabilities from harness craft. Now, here is uh what happened when they tested it on SweepBench Pro. These results are for Opus 4.5, uh which is a couple of generation older, but directionally the results are going to hold. So, it scored 80.9% on SweepBench Verified. On SweepBench Pro, uh with the standard minimal scaffolding, the score was 45.9%. Now, to be fair, SweepBench Pro is also a much harder uh set of tasks. You're looking at multi-file long-running um long time horizon tasks. So, part of the gap is task difficulty. But, here is the part that's purely scaffolding. Take the same Opus 4.5 on the Swebench Pro task. Now, let's different agent systems bring their own custom harnesses. Now, on the same benchmark, Cursor harness gets it up to 50.2%. Claude could harness pushes it to 55.4. That's roughly a 10-point swing for the same model, same task, but simply different scaffolding. So, when Cursor and Anthropic tell you they spend their engineering effort on the harness, this is what they mean. The harness is not just a wrapper. It's the actual multiplier these days. Now, if you thought harness matters this much, what happens when you switch models mid-conversation? This is a pattern I have seen a lot. People usually plan with one model, and implementation is done with another model. Cursor walked walks to this in a section literally called facilitating mid-chat model switching. And here's what they say is hard about it. In their words, "Different models have different behaviors, prompts, and tool shapes." End quote. So, when you switch, the new model has to apply its tools to a conversation history produced by a different model, which is out of distribution from what it was trained on. Now, there's another problem. A cache provider and model specific switching means a cache miss. So, your first turn after the switch is going to be slower and more expensive. Now, here's Cursor's solution. They insert custom instructions that warn um the new model. Hey, uh, you just took over from another model. Some of these tools in the chat history are not yours. Don't call them. That's the engineering work needed for a feature that every uh, provider makes look like a single click. And here's the actual recommendation. We generally recommend staying with one model for the duration of a conversation unless you have a reason to switch. End quote. Now, this is not only limited to cursor. Even for something like Claude code, they recommend not switching between Opus, uh, and Sonnet or Haiku mid-conversation. And the reasons are very similar to what a cursor highlighted. Because every, uh, um, drop-down that lets you switch models mid- task is hiding real cost. You're going to have slower turns, uh, cache misses, and a model operating on context that does not fully understand it. It looks free, but it's not. Now, let's talk about the role of agent harnesses in multi-agent [clears throat] systems. So, Cursor, um, in their blog post, called, uh, this, The Harness and the Future of Software Development. Here is how they describe what's coming in their own words. Quote, instead of running every, uh, subtask through a single agent, the system will learn to delegate across specialized agents and sub-agents. One for planning, another one for fast edits, and the third one for debugging. Now, this sounds great in theory. You can break the work down into pieces and let specialized agents handle each piece. It's an easy win. But, here's the math that you actually need to care about. So, take one agent at 95% reliability, which seems pretty good, right? Now, chain five of them together, a planner, an editor, a debugger, a reviewer, and a tester. Each one uh with 95% reliability. However, at the end of the chain, you're going to get to 77.4% reliability. Because the errors are going to accumulate, your end-to-end reliability just collapsed. So, the work that flowed perfectly through one agent now fails one in four times. This is the compounding problem, and it's why multi-agent systems that look great uh in the beginning starts to fall apart in production systems. So, you need to actually rethink on how to build multi-agent systems. Cursor's answer to this is making that work well is fundamentally uh harness challenge, end quote. The system needs to know which agent to dispatch, how to frame the task for each agent's strength, how to stitch the results into a coherent workflow. Now, in this case, the orchestration logic does not live in a single agent. It lives in the harness. And you need to treat harnesses as an ever-evolving software system. While harness engineering has always been important for agent success, it's only going to be more critical going forward. Which, in translation, the harness was a small piece of agent quality in the past, it's about to be the whole game. So, what does any of this means if you're building an agent or using these tools? So, there are three takeaways that I want you to consider. Number one, if you're building agents, stop treating prompts and tools as glue code. You need to version them, measure them, run AB tests, uh track keep rate or whatever your version of it is. Treat the harness like an actual product because it is becoming one. Number two, if you're picking a model, don't trust headline benchmarks. Ask which harness produced the numbers. The same Opus 45 or 47, we showed use code 80% on one benchmark and 46% on another. It's the same model, different harness, the numbers tells you nothing without the context. Number three, the most important one, your moat as a builder is not model access. Everyone has access to exactly the same model. Your moat is harness craft, the orchestration logic, the context strategy, the error handling, the tool format choices for each provider. Two years ago, the harness was a small piece of agent quality. Now, it's the most important agent quality. So, if you're using agents or building a harness around agents, I think you need to pay close attention to model specific tools and harnesses. This is where you're going to get the most gain. So, in 2026, I think it's the wrong question to ask, uh which model is the best. The right question is which harness around a specific model is the best one. If you're building agentic systems, do let me know in the comment section below. If you like the content, uh subscribe to the channel. I am I'm to be putting out a lot of technical content around agent harnesses, sandboxes, system designs. So, if you're interested in content like this, make sure to subscribe to the channel. Anyways, I hope you found this video useful. Thanks for watching and as always, see you in the next one.

analysis.summary: 视频强调LLM代理性能的关键在于其“Harness”（即工具、上下文管理、错误处理和编排逻辑），而非模型本身。模型与工具格式匹配性、模型切换的弊端以及多代理系统中的可靠性挑战均需通过精心设计的Harness解决。Harness工程是LLM代理成功的核心，是新的竞争优势。

title: RAG is Dead. Again. (Claude Agent SDK + Memory)
description:

Project Github Repo: https://github.com/PromtEngineer/ParseRAG
Signup Zilliz Cloud (managed Milvus) to claim $100 credits with business emails: https://tinyurl.

This system is built on top of the agent SDK, but you can use it with Claude code. It uses some ideas from my agentic file search open-source project that gives your agent a set of simple tools that helps it to retrieve information from both structured and unstructured documents. To show you how it works, here are 16 different documents, each with a very complex layout that combines free-flowing text, tables, and images. Now, here I'm asking a complex question that needs information retrieval from multiple different documents, and then it needs the agent to reason about its results. So, here is how it works. The agent initially makes a plan, reads the folder, then based on that, it identifies the documents that it thinks are going to be interesting. It passes those files, look at those individually, then it does semantic search, and based on all of that analysis and results, it actually comes up with a response that is well-grounded in the documents that we are providing. And it also has the ability to go back if it missed a specific source during its retrieval process. Now, instead of retrieval of information, you can use this system as a memory system for your agent. At the moment, this agent is powered by Claude agent SDK, but you can use the same setup with Claude code or even Codex if you simply create an MCP server or skill around this thing. Now, the memory system has two different components. The first one is semantic similarity based. For this, we're using Milvus, which is a high-performance vector store. More on that later in the video. And then, the second one is simple tools which can use the file system. These tools involve scanning documents in a folder, reading those documents, parsing the documents, and searching for those documents. Think of this as tools that are available to Claude code when it's doing search for specific code segments. Now, we do one more thing. For every page that has a picture, we take a screenshot of that. All of this parsing of the documents is happening through Light Parser, which is an open-source project from Llama Index. And for vector store, I'm using Milvus, which is a high-performance vector store that can scale with your data. Here's the ingestion pipeline, which is going to look very familiar. It's a standard ingestion pipeline with one key difference. For document parsing, we are using Light Parser, which is great for processing complex structures in PDF files. And the idea is that not only it can process text, but you can detect the presence of images, and for those, we actually take screenshots and store them separately. Now, this is helpful because you don't have to take a screenshot of every page. You only take screenshot of the pages that actually have visual content in it, like images or graphs. But you also keep all the text components, which are going to be critical for text-based retrieval. Then, we run through a simple chunking process. In this case, we are using the Gemini embedding as a high-dimensional embedding, and everything is stored in Milvus vector store. Now, let's talk about the data structure that we are saving. Now, here's the simple schema of everything that we are storing inside Milvus. It's great. It can scale up to billions of records. But right now, we are including the source document, the extracted text, the computing computed embedding, then path to the actual image files that are extracted from it, and then also a number of different key metadata that we use for metadata-based filtering. Okay, so why Milvus? This is a full vector database, which is built for scale. It supports both CPU and GPU acceleration, which makes it a very interesting choice. If you're using Milvus light, you can just create a single file that contains everything. But if you need scale, this supports fully distributed Kubernetes native architecture that enables it to scale horizontally, which means you can run tens of thousands of search queries on billions of vectors. And the best part, it's under Apache 2.0, so you can use it for commercial purposes. And it has over 40,000 stars on GitHub. But if you want a hosted solution, they have Zilliz Cloud, which runs Milvus as a fully managed service for you. It's very simple to use, so you'll first create the client, then define the collection name, what is going to be the dimensions of your vector store, and after that, just provide your data for data ingestion. They are also the current sponsors of today's video. Now, the agent itself has two sets of tools. One is powered by Milvus. This is basic semantic similarity web search, and also the agent can get images, which are the screenshots of pages that contains visual information. On the other hand, it has access to file system. Basically, a number of different bash tools which enables the agent to scan a folder, preview different documents, parse them, read the entire document, and look for patterns. These tools are extremely powerful if you're doing any sort of search. Now, reading every document through file system-based tools is going to be pretty expensive. So, that's why we use the indexed path as a pre-filtering. So, we use the semantic similarity search to reduce the search space, to identify the most relevant chunks, and then we use these file system-based tools to read through the documents to which these chunks belong. And in the process, the system is smart enough that it can go back and look at a specific document if it was missed during the initial retrieval process. Now, here's the retrieval pipeline. The user asks a question, we compute embeddings, Milvus does the cosine similarity-based retrieval, we get top chunks, and then Claude through the agent SDK can look at those chunks, reason through it, can find the documents from which these chunks are coming from, read through those documents if it needs to, and backtrack if it thinks that the initial semantic retrieval missed any documents that are relevant. So, here's the GitHub repo. The code is going to be available for you to experiment. Now, this is going to give you an overview of what exactly the different tools are. Here's the main strategy that I tried to explain. Initially, it does a parallel scan, then deep dive, and then backtrack if it needs further information. Also, here are the instructions on how to set it up. I have already set up everything. So, I'm going to just walk you through a few examples of how this system works. Okay, so let me show you some example of how this system works, and it can do long horizon search. Now, for this example, we're using medical data that I have collected from multiple different sources. So, here's from FDA on how to manage high blood pressure. If you look at these documents, they have free-flowing text, tables, images, and there's no single structure, which it makes it very complex. If you use something like DocLing, it is going to have pretty hard time parsing this. So, this is where Llama Parser comes into play. One of the things that it does pretty nicely is that not only it can extract data from tables, but also it can identify images, and then take screenshot of these pages and put them separately. Now, this first question question is about the side effect of a specific medicine. This is something that an agentic rag or retrieval augmented generation system would be able to easily do because if you're using semantic similarity, then it can just look at what exactly this means, and will probably be able to find you the document in the first pass. And this is kind of what happens here as well. Initially, it is thinking about a plan, then runs the query through the semantic search mechanism, and it thinks that, okay, there's this one specific table. It fetches that and after that, it runs another search query to ensure that it has all the information and it can generate the response pretty accurately, which is grounded in the documents that it was looking at. But then, you can run more complex queries. So, let's say if you run a query like compare the diabetes medication guides from FDA versus ADA and what they cover differently. This is something that a normal rag system might have struggled with unless you run it through query decomposition, which initially looks at one guide, then the second one guide, and then have another reasoning step through it to look at the second one. Now, since we mentioned specific guides, so it actually goes scan the documents or scan the folders, identify that specific file, pick up both of these, and then go through a reasoning loop to produce the final answer. Then, you can have more open-ended questions. So, what food interactions should patient on the blood pressure medication be aware of? Now, in this case, the agent decides to have multiple different passes, and you can actually see that for every search query, it keeps changing it based on the context that it's gathering to ensure that it captures a wide variety of information sources. But, there is more you can do here. Uh it has access to a specific set of tools. You can tell the agent to use those tools. Uh so, let's say in this case we're asking for it to look for blood thinners and then scan the document folder to find any additional sources that were not covered in the initial search results. And the process that you see here is that initially it does search, then based on the queries that it generated, it starts parsing different documents and starts reading through them. Now, this one takes about 16 different steps. However, this kind of ensures that it's looking at all the relevant documents and making sure that whatever were missed during the initial retrieval, it's able to capture that. And that's kind of the beauty of this system. You can run pretty complex queries through it. So, this was a quick overview of the project that I'm currently building. I've given Claude agent's persistent memory. The code is open source. A link to the GitHub repo is going to be in the video description. I hope you find this video useful. Thanks for watching and as always, see you in the next one.

title: Tokenize Time: Inside the Interaction Model Architecture.
description:
Thinking Machines just changed the turn-based AI paradigm by introducing a new architecture that tokenizes time into continuous 200ms micro-turns. Let's dive deep into the technical paper to explore their encoder-free early fusion approach and see why it fundamentally changes how we build agentic systems compared to frontier models like GPT-4o and Gemini.

https://thinkingmachines.ai/blog/interaction-models/

My voice to text App: whryte.com
Website: https://engineerprompt.ai/
RAG Beyond Basics

Uh I unfortunately haven't seen much coverage on YouTube, so I thought to create this video. My focus is going to be more on the technical details because that what is interesting to me. Uh but there are some pretty interesting demos that I'm going to show you in this video. But first, let's let's address the elephant in the room. Some people are comparing this with the GPT-4 0 that was released by OpenAI a couple of years ago. Now we have a second version of that. But I think the technology that Thinking Machines is using is very different. The [clears throat] way that they have that trained a unified model that can tokenize time is a very different approach than what things like GPT-4 0's advanced voice mode or even things like Gemini uses and that's what I want to focus in this video. By the way, this person that you see here in the GPT-4 0 demo is the same person that is showing the interaction model demo in Thinking Machines. Most of the team is coming from either DeepMind, OpenAI, or I think there are a couple of people from Anthropic, but these are the people who have worked on similar systems in the past and they are building the next version of these. Okay, so I find this a demo pretty interesting. So let's watch it, but it also shows you the capabilities of this model as well. You're going to translate what I say into HR speech, okay? Exactly. I'll be reframing everything you say into uplifting professional language instantly. Hi Alex, I cannot stand your lateness. Hi Alex, we'd love to explore opportunities to enhance your timeliness and morning engagement. >> You cannot expect the rest of us to rearrange our workflow >> Let's collaborate on a schedule that ensures everyone stays aligned and included in those key Okay, so a couple of things to notice here. Uh, first of all, the model is interacting with the user in real time. And as the user is talking, the model is able to not only produce responses, but it's also able to keep track of what the user is saying, right? So, it's not a multi-turn conversation where in the first turn the user asks something, the model responds, then the user asks something subsequent, and the model is going to respond again. But this is very different. And let me explain how this works. But before that, let's look at what a usual full duplex voice to voice identity system looks like. Now, usually people combine multiple different components together to create these interactive systems that feel like real-time responses from the single model. But in fact, there are multiple different components. So, in a traditional system, you would have a voice activity detection, which detects whenever a user is speaking or they start speaking, right? Then you have a speech to text model that is going to convert your speech into text that is sent to the LLM, which is basically the brain of the system. And then you have a text-to-speech component. And on top of it is a session management or orchestrator layer. And then there is going to be external service services, which basically keeps track of when the user starts talking, right? So, normally there are a lot of different moving parts. Okay? And that's what enables voice multi-turn conversational systems. So, a simple view would be the human has input, the model produces an output, there's a second input, the model produces another output, right? However, they created this whole architecture where it can offload tasks to a background model, which is running asynchronously, and which is much more capable. analysis.summary: DeepSeek V4是一款免费开源LLM，拥有百万级上下文窗口，性能超越Gemini 3.1 Pro，且成本极低。其核心技术是KV缓存的三层压缩（摘要、结构、索引），大幅降低内存需求和计算成本。尽管有单模态和上下文限制，但其在编码等方面表现出色。

title: Rethinking Agents - Harness is All you Need?
description:
Thanks to DataImpluse for sponsoring this video: https://dataimpulse.com/?utm_source=youtube&utm_medium=video&utm_campaign=engineerprompt

Two new papers from Stanford and Tsinghua just put hard numbers on something most agent builders have been feeling — the orchestration code wrapping your LLM now drives more performance variation than the model itself. Same model, six-times the gap, depending entirely on what researchers are calling the harness. If you build agents, the lever you shou
raw_transcript: Okay, so the orchestration code wrapping your LLM now drives more performance variation than the model itself. Same model, six times the performance gap, uh depending entirely on the wrapper or harness around it. That's the headline finding from two papers from Stanford and Tsinghua University. And it means that the entire which model is the best debate has been wrong question for a while now. Now, in this video, I'm going to walk you through three things. First, exactly what a harness is and why it matters more than the model itself now. Second, the two papers from the Tsinghua and Stanford that formalized this as a discipline that is known as harness engineering. And third, the practical takeaways. Uh what specifically you should change about how you build agents starting today. By the end of this video, you will know exactly which levers to pull when your agent is underperforming. And I think you'll find it's almost never the lever you're reaching out for. Okay, so let me break it down for you. In simple terms, a harness is a architecture that turns a model into an agent. The agent itself is just a one-shot text generator. You ask, it answers, and then it stops. The harness is what gives it the ability to take actions, see the consequences, and keep going until the problem is actually solved. Now, there is a really clean way to think about this. And it's an operating system analogy. The raw LLM is the CPU, which is powerful but inert. No memory, no storage, no IO. Your context window acts as RAM, which is fast but limited. External databases serve as disk. Tool integrations are device drivers, and the harness is the operating system, deciding what the CPU sees and when. And I think this analogy actually maps really, really cleanly onto the nine harness components I covered in my previous video. Now, here is why this matters for today's video. My previous video answered what a harness is. What this video is about is something different. Why is the same harness producing six times performance differences depending on exactly how those components are structured. Okay, so let's look at the first paper. And this is where it gets really interesting. The first paper is from Pan and team in Tsinghua University, which was published in March 2026. They asked a simple question, "What if you could write an agent's entire control logic not in Python, not in YAML, but in structured natural language?" The structure has three layers. At the bottom, back end, the actual infrastructure and tools. In the middle, what they call the runtime charter, which is a universal physics basically how contracts bind, how state persists, how uh sub-agents or child agents are managed, and on the top, the natural language agent harness itself, which holds the state-specific logic. These include contracts, roles, state structure, and failure modes. Now, why does this separation matter? It [snorts] gives harness engineering controlled experimentation. So, you can swap the harness while fixing the runtime and you are testing harness design in isolation, which gives you clean ablation. Now, here's uh where the results get interesting. On Sweep and verified with GPT-54 at maximum reasoning, uh the result rate clustered around 74 and 76% regardless of the configuration. But, the full harness burned 16.3 million prompt tokens per sample. You're talking about more than 600 tool calls with more than 32 minutes runtime. But, then this stripped-down a version of this, which used 1.2 million tokens, 51 calls, under 7 minutes, same destination, 14 times the compute for the same result. Uh they did a module by module ablation, which means that they started removing or subtracting parts of the system. Self-evolution was the only consistently helpful module. Verifiers actually start hurting. They saw -0.8 on Sweep Bench, -8.4 on OS World. So, multi-candidate search hurt by 5.6. More structure is not always better, which is kind of a surprising result. But, if you think about it, it kind of makes sense. I'm going to come back to this in the practical takeaways. And but, the headline results from this paper came from a different experiment. They took OS Symphony, uh native code harness for desktop automation, and migrated its logic into natural language representation. Again, they used the same strategy, uh but just rewritten in natural language. And in this case, performance jumped from 30.4% to 47.2%. Run time dropped from 361 minutes to 141 minutes. LLM calls collapsed from 1,200 to 34. The representation itself drove the gain. That's a really, really significant finding. Now, keep this in mind, but let's look at this other paper, which was released by Omar Khattab who built DS by. And they took it one step further. If representation matters this much, can we find the right harness automatically? A quick word from today's sponsor. If you're building production systems that need to access data available on the web, whether it's a rack system or an authentic system, you probably have noticed that as you scale up, some of the requests are going to be blocked. It doesn't matter how good your logic is, if the IP gets flagged, the data stops moving. The fix is to have good proxies. This is where today's sponsor, DataImpulse, comes into play. The residential pool is honestly hard to beat on value. Pricing starts at a dollar a gigabyte, and what you buy does not expire. On top of that, over 90 million ethically sourced IPs across 195 different countries. If you're already running Scrapy, Playwright, or Puppeteer, they have got full integration guides on each one right in their docs. So, you can wire their proxies straight into the applications that you're already building. And if your app needs mobile traffic, you can swipe right over that. There is a dedicated mobile pool, and your script barely changes. You're using the same auth, same call, a different endpoint, and you're routing through a phone class IP. Link is going to be in the description. If your shipping a data pipeline needs IPs in specific city, or you just want scrapping to stop being a daily fight, take a look. Now, back to the video. Again, it's structured retrieval memory orchestration topology. It controls everything. Now, here's how the loop works. The agentic proposal, uh in this case they used Claude Opus 4 6, reads failed execution traces, diagnoses what broke, and rewrites the complete new harness. The final scores and raw traces accumulate in a growing file system, and the loop repeats. The scale here is what makes this work. Uh we're looking at 10 million tokens per iteration. 400 times more feedback than any prior method. It's reading about 82 files per round, and those raw traces are irreplaceable. When they removed them, uh accuracy dropped from 50% to 34%. When they replaced traces with summaries, 34.9%. This signal lives in the raw details. If you summarize prior failures and just feed those into the model, it could uh hurt the performance of your agent. Now, the results ranked two with Opus, ranked with one with Haiku. So, in this case a smaller model outranking larger ones through harness optimization alone. Uh the numbers are 76.4% on Terminal Bench 2. The only auto-optimized system in a field of hand engineer entries on a 215 text classification it was scoring 7.7 points above state of the art using four times fewer tokens. But here's the finding that I think is going to be significant. A harness optimized on one model transferred to five other models in improving them all. So think about this. And what exactly does this mean? The reusable asset is not the model, it's the harness. You can build it once, it works across the model landscape. Now, I don't want to generalize it too much. Now, this all might sound academic until you map it onto something you have probably already noticed yourself. Take any frontier model, say Claude Opus 46 or 47, GPT 54, or even Gemini. Doesn't matter which. Run it the exact same prompt through it inside Claude Code, then Cursor, then Anti-Gravity. And the idea is that you keep the model exactly the same, you just change the harness. Now, the prompt needs to be relatively complex, but you're going to see noticeably different results. You're going to see different reasoning paths with different token spans and potentially different success rates. And all of this is driven by not the model, but the harness that you're using with the agent. Opus 47 behaves very differently in Claude Code than inside Cursor or other third-party harnesses like Open Code. Okay. So, the most important practical takeaway from all of this, and the one I think most builders are getting wrong, is what Anthropic calls the subtraction principle. So, every harness components and remember in my last video, we walked through nine of them encodes an assumption about what the model cannot do alone. And those assumption expires when the models improve. When Opus 4 6 stopped needing context resets, Anthropic just dropped them entirely. Manas, the agent platform, rewrote their harness five times in six months. Wessel removed 80% of the agents tool and got better results. That is pretty significant. Now, here is what it means. Mature harness work looks less like building structure up and more like pruning it down. In the last year, the idea was to provide agents more and more tools, but that is going away. Simpler is much better. A craft of subtraction as much as addition. Shin Kua ablation we just looked at really proves this. Sometimes the verified can actually hurt your agent a lot more than it can help. The way you want to think about it is that if the model is able to do uh an aspect that was handcrafted in the harness before, you want to simplify your harness and drop that specific tool. Now, uh if you are building an agent, you are a harness engineer, whether you call yourself or not. So, when you run into something that is not working, here is the order of operations I would suggest. Don't switch the model first. Audit the harness. And here are four questions that you can ask yourself. One, what's in your context window that does not need to be there? Two, which tool do you have that the agent rarely uses well? Three, are you adding verification or search loops that might be hurting performance like the Xinhua University found? And four, is your control logic written in code or in language? Because the migration experiment we looked at before showed almost 17 points gained just from rewriting the same logic. And it's no longer a question of which model to pick, it's a question of which structure to remove. Anyways, I hope you found this video useful. Thanks for watching and I'll see you in the next one.

analysis.summary: 该视频指出，LLM代理的性能主要取决于其外部封装（harness），而非模型本身。斯坦福和清华大学的研究表明，通过优化harness可实现显著性能提升，如将控制逻辑用自然语言表达、或自动优化harness。实践中，应遵循“减法原则”，即简化harness，移除不必要的组件和工具，而非盲目增加复杂性。
analysis.speaker: Unknown

analysis.summary: 该视频分析了评估AI进展的“时间地平线”指标，详细阐述了其方法论，现有基准测试的挑战（如数据污染、捷径、泛化能力），以及使用人类完成任务时间作为统一且可解释的AI能力衡量标准的重要性。讨论还涉及AI安全与对齐问题，特别是模型虽知正确行为却仍出现不良表现，以及奖励作弊的风险。
analysis.speaker: Beth Barnes & David Rein

raw_transcript: One of the biggest problem with deepseek v4 was that it's texton model but seems like some users are getting access to the vision version and now they also released a paper titled thinking with visual primitives and then it kind of disappeared. The headline numbers are genuinely wild. So for an 80x 80 resolution image, this new model uses about 90 entries in its KV cache. In comparison, Sonnet 4.6 uses around 870 whereas Gemini 3 flash uses,00 that's almost 10 times less. So, it seems like they have figured out how to create and serve extremely efficient vision models. So, in this video, I want to do three things. First, I'll walk you through where this fits in deepseeks vision lineage because this is not really a one-off paper. They have been telling the same story for 2 years now. Second, I'll explain the actual idea which they call thinking with visual primitives because it's a really really clean concept once you see it. And third, we will look at the architecture, the benchmark numbers and the limitations that they are not hiding. Okay, so let's start with the lineage. Deepseek has shipped roughly seven vision related models in the last 24 months. Back in March of 2024, they put out DeepSeek VL. It was a small modest 1.3 and 7 billion parameter models. Uh they were using hybrid SIG lip and SAM encoder. uh which was nothing flashy at the time but it set the foundation. Then in October 2024 they released Janice. This one mattered architecturally. Uh they decoupled the visual encoder for understanding versus generation. Most unified multimodal model at the time had this single encoder bottleneck where the model was forced to compromise. Janus basically said no and the idea was to run two encoders but share the transformer. In December 2024 they released a second version of the vision language model. This is where the efficiency story really starts. This is the first version and the framing was strange because they called it OCR but it really wasn't. The actual idea was that take thousand text tokens, render them as an image, encode the image and you get back 100 vision tokens that encode to the original text at 97% accuracy which is 10 times compression on long context and Karpathi reacted to it like this quote the tokenizer must go pixel may be better inputs to language models than text. End quote. That's a moment a lot of people started talking about DeepS vision Tim. Seriously. So if you ask uh what the through line is across all these models, it's basically one question. What's the cheapest representation that still works? So V2 uh said fewer activated parameters in Genus. They said decouple the encoders. In the OCR paper, uh they said compress text into pixels. And this paper says make special coordinates first class tokens in the chain of thought. Okay, so let's get into what the paper is actually arguing. There are two gaps in the current multimodal models. The first one is the perception gap. The model cannot see finer details. And we have seen some example from Frontier Labs like with the nano banana models with the open AI image generation models. But the Deep Seek team is arguing that there is a second more fundamental gap they call the reference gap. Even with the model sees perfectly, language is too imprecise to point. But you will lose track of which entity you are actually talking about as your reasoning gets longers. Now humans solves this with uh finger gestures. Models until now didn't have that equivalent. Every single person has a coordinate and the format is just inline tokens. The model literally outputs a reference tag with a label followed by a box tag with two corner coordinates. These are special tokens in the model's vocabulary. It's not function calling. It's not a separate tool. It's part of the chain of thought. You can actually see why this is really really powerful idea. Counting in dense scenes, multihop special reasoning, and even the famous Chihuahua versus muffin meme. It all becomes more reliable when the model can literally point to things. All right. Uh so let's look at how the model is built because the efficiency angle is genuinely impressive as with other DeepSeek models. The base architecture is pretty standard. April 29, uh, DeepSeek started rolling out vision mode in the app and on the web, uh, at least a limited test along their fast and expert modes. Now the bigger story here honestly is uh what's underneath this model and that is Deepseek V4 flash the paper sites uh V4 as reference number three highly efficient million token context intelligence. raw_transcript: So, Open AI just released this open-source repo called Symphony. It's a totally different way of interacting with agents. So, the way we use coding agent has shifted a lot for the past few months. From initially just the auto-complete to primarily interactive session with coding agent to now most of us around two or three different sessions in parallel, each working isolated work tree for different features or bug fixing. And then new tooling like Super set or conductor that has been introduced to help you run and manage different interactive coding sessions easier. The problem is that even with those tools, many people, including myself, will feel this burden when we are working on more than like three different sessions cuz we just can't context switch every minute. And I personally have had multiple times sending the wrong instruction to the wrong thread. So, ceiling of how much we can get out from those coding agent is no longer the model capability, but our own attention and cognitive load. And the recent project Symphony is so interesting is that Open AI's engineering team had this realization that the current experience has been orienting around coding session, merge PRs, but in reality for the past decades, software workflow are largely organized around deliverables, things like issues, tasks, tickets, milestones. Engineer leaders have been managing massive amount of tasks across thousands of workers, not by reviewing everyone's PR, but looking at final outcomes using tools like linear and Atlassian. And Open AI's proposed solution is move human up a level. Instead of managing two three interactive sessions, you manage tickets. The agent works at ticket level, report back through the ticket itself, and you stay in the loop without monitoring individual sessions. The ticket tracker becomes state machine itself. And the way Symphony makes this work is almost embarrassingly simple, but very effective. It's a background process. You run it once, point to a workflow file, which we'll talk a bit more, and then it runs forever. Every 30 seconds, this background process will glance through your linear board. But they have this spec.md file that's detailing how this framework or system is designed. So, you can just drop this file to any coding agent and ask it to build and design a system in any programming language. There are already a lot of different community attempts. And I'm going to show you step by step how you can set these things up. But orchestrating agent is only part of the work. As Open AI mentioned, this whole thing only works if your coding agent's environment is set up properly in a way that it can complete tickets end-to-end atomically, which you can call it harness engineer, but fundamentally just whether your environment or code base has been set up in the right way, so agent has everything it needs to complete task end-to-end. And does the system has a proper documentation structure for different things. And I think most people does have these two things properly set up in your code.md or agent.md file. But the part I think most of team didn't set up is those self-verifying tools. They allow agent to do an end-to-end test after implementing something. And even submit a video recording to prove that it have tested and it's working in the ticket directly just like in their demo. But in the doc, they didn't really mention how they were handling this part. So, I did some research across many major skills. And the best one I found is this Playwright CRI tool. So, I believe many of us are pretty familiar with Playwright MCP, which allow agent to use the browser and do a task, check the logs. But the problem before was that Playwright with MCP setup, it took a huge amount of tokens in context window even when it's not needed. But they have released this Playwright CRI tool alongside agent skill that detailing every single comment. And the most interesting comment is this video recording CRI. So, Playwright allow agent to run commands like video start and video stop to capture browser session into a MP4 or WebM video. They even have some pretty advanced video rendering capability where they can add different chapter on the screen. Like here's one example video where it can record its own session and even add new HTML element on top of the screen to annotate the action the agent took. And then upload session into linear, so you can very easily verify if things actually work. And as far as I know, other tools like Chrome DevTools MCP or agent browser don't have this video capability out of box. So, this is one very important skill that will make your whole experience complete. And meanwhile, there are also other skills that you should add. And I just take one of the repo I have as example. We have this Playwright CRI tool that has a skill as well as a list of reference for agent to know how to like record a video and tracing the debug logs. And we also have a skill here to tell agent how to start server locally. And because ours is pretty straightforward, so it's just a skill file. But sometimes for more complicated things, you can create predefined script as well. So, agent no longer spends cognitive power on those type of stuff. And meanwhile, I also created this linear skill that allow agent to know how to operate linear tickets by using linear API as well as things like upload video evidence of the test. And we actually have more documentations about different parts of system. And in the agent.md or cloud.md file, this is where we have a proper index of different documentation systems, so you can always go and find the relevant information. We also give more detailed debugging skills. For example, we use Grafana to track and store all the logging in production. And we add a relevant Grafana log skill in our repo, so the agent can fetch real production logs for bug fixing. And all those things are try to serve one purpose, which is setting up your code base so that your agent can fix bug, building new features, verify things are working fully atomically end-to-end. And here's basically what I did in Python folder. I just point to spec.md file and ask it to build a new version in Python. But most of the time, you actually don't need to do that. You can just reuse what Open AI provided. And firstly, you can confirm whether the script is So, you can run script by doing this, which point to the Symphony program that has been built. And run help. So, this should show you the actual command about how to run Symphony. You basically just do Symphony and point to a path to workflow.md file. And by default, you can't just run the Symphony like this. You can run this to bind Symphony command to the specific path. So, just run this. And then you can do Symphony, point to a specific workflow.md file. And by default, it will give you this warning. Then you can add this argument to the command, which will set our Symphony background process like this. It will track all the tasks, show you the project, and next refresh time. It will track a specific linear project you set up every 30 seconds. If there any ticket in to-do, it will pick up and show up in this list. And all those configurations are actually defined in workflow.md file. So, in workflow.md file, at the front matter, there is a project slug. And Symphony script will basically read that metadata, importing information from a specific project. Same thing for all the other configurations, like how frequently it should pull the ticket data, what are things it should do after setting up a new workspace, how many agent can be run at the same time, and the Code X configuration. But once you set up this, it's basically monitoring the specific Symphony repo with Elixir implementation. What we want to do is apply this to your own workspace. It's actually pretty straightforward. You can just open any coding agent like Code X or Cloud Code, point to the spec.md file and say, "I want to set up Symphony for my repo, and we will reuse the Elixir implementation here, and help me build the workflow.md file for my repo." With just one command, coding agent is smart enough to look at your own repo and design a workflow.md file inside there. And this is the one it created for me, including the project slug and API key and all the other configurations. But you do need to set up linear first. If you haven't created linear account yet, just go create a one and then add a new project. And in this project, click on the button here, you can just paste into your coding agent. This thing in the middle here is a project slot, or you can manually paste into the workflow.md file as well. And meanwhile, you need to get a linear API key, which you can get by clicking on settings, security and access, and add a new personal API key here. And once you did that, you should run this command, which will save the linear API key globally on your computer. So, every time when agent try to use linear, it can access any projects you have access to. And there are some configuration you should do, which is status. So, Symphony out-of-box are designed for some specials status control flow, like human review status and also merging status. Once you put a ticket into do, Symphony will automatically pick up and put that in progress and trigger an agent session. And once agent finish the work, it will change to human review status, so that you can review the work. And once finished, you can set the status to be merging, which will trigger the agent automatically raise a PR from this work. And once you did all that, you can do run Symphony past through your workflow.md file, plus this I understand that this will be running without the usual guardrail comment. And now Symphony will be working and picking up all the tickets in your project here. To make it easier, you can also create a new view, set up this board, so that you get this kind of Kanban experience. But to just test, I can just create ticket change the landing page hero copy from your company on autopilot to your AI growth team, and the set up the status to be to do. raw_transcript: I love this work. This comes from the Sakana AI Lab in Tokyo and it feels like mad science in the best possible way. We are going to play God in a tiny digital universe. Look, we have five AI species in a Petri dish fighting for territory and nothing. Why is that? Well, this world is brutal. Or AI wrapper companies, killer new social media apps, same deal. Okay, enough of this. Instead, let's be a good God. So, what happens? Look. Woah! Empires start to grow and grow and oh, but too quickly. These empires grow out of nothing quickly and then crumble just as quickly. Why? Because the bar for survival is too low. So, we tighten the screws again. Easy money dries up. We get a tougher economy, and look, all these companies that got addicted to the easy money suddenly disintegrate. Gone. And then, what happens? Look, new ones appear that are more adapted to this environment. And as we start playing with some of these variables, we change the environment and can almost even create one ugly monopoly or an unstable ecosystem. I love this. This is so cool. So, how does it work? Well, here are some basic rules. The paper calls these species neural cellular automata. This is a living, trainable pixel world inside a 2D grid. It gets pretty involved, but here's the gist of it. Each organism competes for these pixels. They can grow from nearby territory and must stay alive by local support. But, here's the key. They are constantly learning. Oh, and they can also fight. They can specify which direction to attack in and which direction to defend. Kind of like having a sword and a shield pointed to a direction. In the actual work, this happens in a higher dimension. You see a simplification of that here. And when species meet at a border, the system calculates which cell defeats which one. What I absolutely love about this work is whether we create a world of total chaos or a stable and healthy ecosystem, it only depends on us. Change the environment and you change the winners. And it decides who rises, who falls, and who never gets a chance. Nature works like this. Markets works like this. Everything works like this. This really shows how important it is that we have good policy in a country, too. Most of the time, you let competition do its work and find the winners. But, sometimes one tiny push makes the whole system healthier. But, it gets even more beautiful. We talked about competition. But, what about collaboration? Is that also possible? I thought of course not, because each organism has one single objective, grow. This creates extreme competition, not collaboration, right? Well, I was completely wrong. Dear fellow scholars, this is Two Minute Papers with Dr. Karoly Zsolnai Feher. Step number one. They call it permissive mixing. I call it growth. We create an environment that is very forgiving. Digital species run wild and spread out everywhere. [clears throat] Then, they bump into each other constantly. We get a big soup. No firm borders. Step number two, crystallization. Now, we raise the threshold a bit. The rules get stricter. To survive this harsh world, species must group up into dense solid shapes to stay alive. Competition draws the borders. Everyone finds out who can live next to whom. Borders harden. Then, step number three, relaxation. We ease into a more forgiving market. Now what? Wow! This is amazing. We get stripes and little checkerboard patterns between these empires. Why? What happened? Well, in the previous stage, weak borders died immediately. It created borders like when we have two different colors of paint drying while there is masking tape between them. Colors separate. Now, hold onto your papers, fellow scholars, because in stage three, we remove the masking tape. What happens? Well, the edges break open. They flow into each other creating these little streaks. These empires are forced to coexist because the game is unable to kill weak border cells so easily. Both sides can keep tiny pieces of land at the edge instead of one side fully erasing the other. Beautiful, simple, brilliant. But this is also an incredible life lesson. If you're always too loose, your life becomes a soup. And if you're always too strict, your life becomes a prison. I am guilty of this, too. This is not the way. Instead, start out loose. Give yourself room to find your way. Then, build discipline. Harden up. Find your shape. Establish boundaries. And finally, do not stay frozen forever. Loosen up a bit. Grow. Adapt. Let new things in. Beautiful life advice in a beautiful paper. Try it out for free in the video description. It is amazing. What a time to be alive. And if you enjoyed this, subscribe and hit the bell so you get more papers. And more papers is always better. Thank you. Here you see me running the full Deep Seek AI model through Lambda GPU Cloud. 671 billion parameters running super fast and super reliably. This is insane. I love it and I use it on a regular basis. raw_transcript: Everybody talks about agent harnesses, but what exactly is a harness? Now, even people who are actively building agents can't always give you a clean answer. Uh the word gets thrown around constantly, but nobody really agrees on what exactly it means. So, in this video, I want to do three things. First, define what a harness actually is. And [snorts] just as more importantly, what it's not. Then, we will walk through nine components that I think makes a modern harness. And finally, we'll build a tiny one in Python, so you can see exactly what is going inside. This is going to be especially important for people who are thinking about building agents and harnesses. In simple term, a harness is a fixed architecture that turns a model into an agent. So, if you think about modern LLMs or models, these are just one-shot text generators. You ask a question, it answers and stops. A harness is what gives it the ability to take action, see the consequences, and keep going until the problem is actually solved. So, think of a model as the engine and the harness around it as the car. That what's make an agent. So, a really good example of this is agentic coding tools like Codex, Cursor, uh Cloud Code, Windswept. These are all harnesses. Each one started from a concrete problem making a model write and edit code across a real repository. And I think they have uh converged on remarkably similar architectures. Now, we're going to look at that architecture in a minute, but first, I want to talk about something else that you probably have heard about. And this is frameworks. So, think about things like LangChain, LangGraph, AutoGen, CrewAI. These are not harnesses. And I think this distinction is really worth making because right now, people are using these terms interchangeably. And it's kind of getting confusing. So, a framework gives you abstraction. Uh think about state crafts, chains, memory connections, and retrievers. You as a user have to wire them together. The fundamental assumption is that you, the human architect, will configure these pieces together. Now, harness on the other side uh is from opposite direction. There's no assembly step. It basically uh ships a working agent. And in simple terms, it's just a while loop with a tool registry and permission layer. And everything comes wired together. Now, another way to think about this is that framework is built for a human to assemble an agent. A harness is built for the agent itself to a task. And in big picture, you just provide the goal, the harness will handle the rest. So, in the rest of the video, we're going to primarily focus on harnesses and what they make them interesting. Okay, so what exactly is inside a harness? I would say there are nine main components that you need to consider if you're building an agentic harness. Now, we're going to uh go through the list. This is mostly opinionated uh architecture, uh but something that I have seen to work really nicely in practice. I'll try to tie together to Cloud Code because I think this is um an example of a really great harness put together. Okay, so the first component is the while loop. Um this is basically the foundation. It's the outer iteration loop. The harness is at its core of while loop. The model reads its system uh prompt, decides which tool to call, runs the tool, feeds the result back into the context, and loops again. And this process keeps repeating till the uh model produces a text-only response or it uh hits a maximum iteration cap. Now, we're talking about uh text-only models, but the same can apply to multimodal models as well. So, think of this outer loop as the whole engine that runs everything. Now, number two is context management. On every turn, the tree grows um as you encounter more uh user messages, more tool calls, more you're going to see that uh you hit the context limit of your large language model. So, the harness has to decide what to keep verbatim, what to summarize, and what to throw away. In Cloud Code's example, the budget used to be around 200,000 tokens. Now, they have increased it to 1 million token in case of Opus. Uh but let's say when you are uh reaching almost half of it, um or maybe 80 to 90%, it triggers a compaction. Some of the most recent messages are going to stay in full. Everything older gets summarized. Now, this compaction is very important um and it can have some real bad consequences if not um properly did. So, you need to be very careful about context management. Now, the third component uh is skills and tools. So, tools are the primitives that reads a file, edit a file, run bash, search code. Skills are a top uh a layer on the top. So, they are how organizational knowledge gets encoded. Uh usually, you're going to see them in model files. Now, to think about uh tools and skills, I would say tools are universal. Uh skills are specific to your team, your workflow. And then, there is the registry. Uh so, it tells what is available, uh what permission each uh thing needs, how the call gets dispatched. Now, the fourth component is sub-agent management. Now, at some point um a task gets too big or too parallel for a single conversation thread. So, the harness uh is going to create sub-agents that work in isolation. Each sub-agent gets its own session, its own restricted set of tools, and a focused system prompt that says, uh "You're working on this specific task." Now, the idea over there is to span, restrict, and collect uh the outputs. That's kind of the pattern you want to use. If you want to learn about generative AI in a structured way, you will love today's sponsor, which is Coursera. They have a number of different courses on generative AI. This is where you learn the actual patterns, chain of thought, few-shot, persona prompting, etc. If you're a developer, jump to their generative AI with large language models built with AWS. It's a hands-on lab. You fine-tune, deploy, and ship real LLM applications. And if you want a full career on-ramp, IBM's AI developer professional certificate is for you. You get to build chatbots, apps, RAG, and agents. Right now, get 40% off off 3 months of Coursera Plus through the link in the description. Now, back to the video. Number five is built-in skills. Uh it receives the tool name, the input, and can allow, deny, or modify the call. A post-tool hook uh runs after and can inspect the results. So, the protocol is kind of structured. Uh think about uh a JSON file with exit codes for allow or deny. Now, the beauty is that hooks also enable uh intercommunication between different har- and hooks are how enterprises today adopt harnesses themselves. Now, let's talk about number nine, permissions and safety. So, this is the layer that makes the difference between a useful tool and a dangerous one. Modern harnesses define a hierarchy of permission modes. You can have read-only space right, um full access. Each tool declares the minimum permission it requires. Now, the job of the harness is to enforce that at dispatch time before the tool even um ever runs. And for tools like bash, the harness even classifies the commands dynamically. So, let's say uh if you say list files, uh it's going to be read-only. Uh if you want to delete something, that will need full access. And uh the harness figures it out by parsing the command string. On top of the static permission, you get interactive approvals. The agent can pause, ask, "Should I run this?" right? So, before executing anything dangerous, you want to have this safety layer built into the harness. All right, so these are uh the nine uh components that I think every uh harness needs to have. Iteration loop, context management, skills and tools, sub-agents, built-in uh skills, session persistence or memory, system prompt assembly, life cycle hooks, and permissions. Now, the easiest way to actually understand um a harness is to build one. So, let's write a minimal version of Python. Um Nothing fancy. Just enough to see all these components together. Okay, so in this part, we're going to quickly go over a reference implementation. Uh so, think of this as a structure or template that you want to use if you're building a harness. Okay, so the main engine is the while loop uh that basically controls everything. It assembles the system prompt and starts looping. Now, on every iteration, um the context is going to be compacted if it grows too large. All of these things plus the tool calls and uh calling to sub-agents are going to be implemented within this while loop. You also want to cap how many iterations are going to be in this loop so that it never runs forever. This is really the entire engine. Uh every other file in the project exists to support these few lines. Now, uh this code implements simple context management. Um In in this very simple form, we are just doing compaction. So, if the history uh grows beyond a certain point, we just summarize uh some of the older conversations and put them together. Now, there are more advanced uh compaction techniques, but this is a very simple reference implementation. Now, if you're making tool calls, you also need to decide whether you're going to bring in everything that is done within the tool call or only the input and outputs. So, those are the design design decisions that you'll have to take as an architect. Okay, this code uh implements a simple tool and skills registry. So, every tool in the harness is described by a small um data class, a name, what type of permissions they're going to have, and a handler function, and one line line um description. The registry is just a dictionary that maps the tool name to that record. Now, there are a few functions. Uh calling register adds a new tool. Calling get uh retrieves one for dispatch. Calling descriptors return a lightweight version of the list, which is going to contain name, permissions, description that we're going to send to the model so it knows what is available. Skills are registered um the exact same way. They're just tools whose handle handler uh reads a markdown file at invocation time. Next is sub-agents. Uh so, you can implement multiple different sub-agents. This code looks at three different things for uh exploration, general, and then verification. Now, each archetype has its own permission levels, its own restricted tool list, and its own focus uh system prompt. Now, every uh harness also needs to have built-in primitives. Uh these are the non-negotiable tools every coding harness must ship with. Uh example of this would be reading files, running bash commands. Now, this also depends on uh the type of work you want your agent to perform. Now, in this case, one thing to keep in mind um these primitives need to use pure standard libraries. You don't want to rely on uh framework dependencies um which is going to be critical because that actually enables the model to take actions. Okay, so here is a simple reference implementation for um session memory or uh context uh persistence. Now, every event the agent generates gets written to disk as one line of JSON. You can also use markdowns, but JSON seems to be um the better choice. The append method opens the file in uh append mode, writes the event, and immediately flushes it. That way, if the process crashes after the next line, this one is already safe on disk. The replay method reads the uh file back line by line and reconstructs the full session. Because the file is append-only, uh two runs of the harness can share the same log without stepping on each other. If the harness dies, the file does not. This is the whole durability story. Uh if you want to persist memory, Okay, uh next one is system prompt assembly. Uh so, you don't have to have a fixed system prompt. You can actually dynamically uh load things into the system prompt. So, for example, uh you can load agents.md, cloud.md, or any other memory files that you have stored into the system prompt dynamically just by reading a directory uh and reading files from disk. One thing to uh be aware of, the order matters here. So, keep the static part first, uh and then dynamically load content second. Otherwise, you're going to break the prefix caching. Okay, so next one is hooks, uh which are used for extensibility. Now, there are two different types of hooks. Uh one is pre-tool hook, and the other one is post-tool hook. And the idea is the pre-tool hook fires before any tool runs and can um either allow or deny the call. A post-tool hook fires after the tool runs and sees the output. Uh It cannot block anything. It's there to audit. Uh can be used for logging and observability. Okay, the last component is permissions um and safety. So, each tool declares the minimum permissions it needs. Uh it can be read, workspace, or full. Uh now, the harness needs to uh provide that extensibility and uh control the permissions of every tool. Now, there's one more thing that you need to be aware of. Uh the same tool can be safe or dangerous depending on the command. Uh so, we uh classify it dynamically. Safe commands like list, uh concatenation, and grep uh stay at read-only. Dangerous commands like uh delete, pseudo, or shutdown jump straight to the full access. So, anything else gets workspace level. On top of these static rules, the agent can also pause and ask the user for explicit approval before running anything destructive. And this is the part that you need to implement within your harness. Now, these are the components that I think every harness needs to have. Now, do let me know your thoughts. What are uh components your harness have? And if you're interested in technical contents like this, make sure to subscribe to the channel. Anyways, thanks for watching and I'll see you in the next one.
