title: Solved: The Bug That Haunted AI Video For Years
description:
❤️ Check out Lambda here and sign up for their GPU Cloud: https://lambda.ai/papers

📝 The paper is available here:
https://research.nvidia.com/labs/sil/projects/MOTIVE/

Our Patreon if you wish to support us: https://www.patreon.com/TwoMinutePapers

🙏 We would like to thank our generous Patreon supporters who make Two Minute Papers possible:
Adam Bridges, Benji Rabhan, B Shang, Cameron Navor, Charles Ian Norman Venn, Christian Ahlin, Eric T, Fred R, Gordon Child, Juan Benet, Michael Tedder, Owen
raw_transcript: Today, generating eye-poppingly high-quality videos just by writing a text prompt is possible. You can also get exceptional controllability as well. Also, you can generate three movies that look completely different but land on the same ending. Almost anything you can think of becomes achievable, effortless, and inexpensive. Now, how they are kind of taking over the internet is another story, but pretty much all of these systems have a huge problem. What is the problem? Is it issues with photorealism? No. In photorealism, these AIs are second to none. I am a light transport researcher by trade. I like to write programs that create photorealistic images, and I feel that many of their results are nearly impeccable. I spent more than a decade trying to learn this craft, and these AI systems are picking it up at an incredible speed. That is absolutely crazy, but not so fast. What about motion? Well, now we got a problem. Motion breaks the spell. The frame looks right, but the movement feels wrong. And at this point, most AI researchers just say, "No problem. Just give it more training data, more compute, and we are done." Let's actually test that. This is the base amount of compute for OpenAI's Sora from 2 years ago. Base amount of compute. Yuck. This is not great. And if you look closer, I think you shouldn't, but if you do, you notice that this is what nightmares are made of. Now, if we add four times more compute, we get this. Perfect? Not even close, but the trend is shouting at us. Now, with 32 times more compute, we get this. Now we're talking. The results start to sing. So, case closed, right? If the motion is not good, and if you don't have more compute, uh because who does these days? Well, then, let's just add more training data. Let it look and learn some more. Except that this is completely wrong. This is what this paper is about. When we see an AI generate motion, they developed a technique that is able to ask, "Okay, little AI, where did you learn that?" >> [laughter] >> I love [clears throat] that. Let me give you an example. A foam cube floating on water. And it gives us waves crashing over a pier, surfing, and splashing ocean waves. This is so cool. So, this is where the knowledge came from. But wait. They say that if there are positive examples for your learning, I wonder what negative samples look like. Oh, this makes sense. These are really the worst for learning. Why? Because cartoons, for instance, teach completely conflicting information about physics. In cartoons, characters pause midair before falling, maybe even holding a tiny little umbrella. Bodies bounce like rubber and snap back into their original shape a moment later. Fun for us, not so fun for an AI model trying to learn real physics. Wait a second. I have an idea. What if we don't just put in more training data? What if we give it less? Cut out those bad influences. Can it do better? Well, let's try it out together. Yes, with the base model, we get a coin which is spinning around the wrong axis. And now, hold on to your papers, fellow scholars, because here comes the magic. After cutting out these bad influences, plus fine-tuning the AI with the good ones, look at that. That is a beautiful spinning coin. And I got to say, I was a bit less impressed by the ball example. Yes, the new one is better, but we have seen plenty of systems pull off this kind of movement. In any case, we are fellow scholars here. We don't hand out medals for a couple cherry-picked examples. No. We are more rigorous than that. We look at the research paper. Does the paper deliver? Oh, yes. Yes, it does. I look at the user study and see that it lands the punch. They asked people to judge whether the new or previous method was better. They did it across 50 videos and 17 participants. That is 850 little tests. And drum roll, it has a 74.1% win rate over the original. That is stunning. Okay, so how on earth did they do that? Can we catch an AI in the act of remembering? Is that even possible? And what does that mean for us? Dear fellow scholars, this is Two Minute Papers with Dr. Karoly Zsolnai-Feher. Now, that's a late cold open. All right. Now, they did two things to ensure that this concept works properly. One, you need to be able to separate how things move from how things look. To do that, they introduced a motion masking step through a technique we call optical flow. An old idea, but works great for tracking the path of points over a video. Good call. But here is the genius part. They don't apply this mask to the video itself. Nope. Instead, they apply that mask to the internal learning signals of the AI. This helps them discover where decisions are coming from. Genius idea? Yes, but unfortunately, two, there is a huge problem with this. What is the problem? Well, modern AI models have over 1 billion parameters. Storing and comparing the full learning signals for thousands of videos just takes too much computer memory and time. That's crazy town. Not feasible. Instead, they found a way to, get this, compress down these more than a billion numbers into Excuse me? Am I seeing correctly? That's right. 512. Down from more than a billion. And the results are almost the same. Wow, that is insane. The technique they use is called the Johnson-Lindenstrauss projection, and it was used in Google's Turbo Quant Compression algorithm as well. That is the one to ease memory constraints of large language models on your GPU. What does it do? What it does is it shrinks high-dimensional data into a tiny space, but in a way that it preserves the relative distance between these numbers. Picture a wooden chair. The chair lives in 3D. The shadow lives in 2D. Now, the shadow needs much less data. And if the scene is set up right, the distance between the four chair legs remains exactly the same. And that means that the projection allows us to retain important properties of the data, but cut away a lot of fat. And all this is put together to achieve one thing, to be able to find what videos influenced the AI's decisions, and then to cut away all the junk knowledge. And that is also super important for our thinking. You see, there are topics where I hoped that the more I read, the smarter I would get. Read more, grow wiser. Not true. There are many areas where the more I read, the more I found that I just got stupider. It took me years and years to find out that there are topics where you can read and learn all you want. If the quality of information is low, it does not educate. Instead, it deforms your thinking. So, what is the solution? Well, you need to be able to separate the real from the fantasy. You don't need more, you need less, and you need better. Like you saw in the paper, truth is the best teacher. And you don't need a lot of it. This technique just showed that a tiny clean signal beats a mountain of junk. Slow down. Don't take everything in. Try to verify what you actually hear, and try to take in less. To me, this is the main message of the paper. Brilliant work, brilliant lesson. Love it. And they promise that we'll get the code for free. What a time to be alive. Here you see me running the full Deep Seek AI model through Lambda GPU Cloud. 671 billion parameters running super fast and super reliably. This is insane. I love it, and I use it on a regular basis. Lambda provides you with powerful Nvidia GPUs to run your own chatbots and experiments. Seriously, try it out now at lambda.ai/papers, or click the link in the description.

analysis.summary: 视频探讨了AI视频生成中运动缺陷的根源——不良训练数据（如卡通）。该研究提出通过光学流分离运动与外观，并利用Johnson-Lindenstrauss投影技术高效识别并去除模型内部学习信号中的“坏影响”。这显著提升了视频运动质量，强调了数据质量优于数量，并提及该压缩技术对大型语言模型内存优化的重要性
analysis.speaker: Dr. Karoly Zsolnai-Feher

title: Save 98% on AI Agent Tokens With This One Trick
description:
Thanks to BrightData for sponsoring this video. Checkout their new MCP server here: https://github.com/brightdata/brightdata-mcp

MCP servers can burn through half your context window on tool definitions alone — sometimes 150K tokens before your agent sends a single message. This video walks through 10 techniques (including Anthropic's new Code Execution and Tool Search) that cut MCP token usage by up to 98%.

https://docs.brightdata.com/ai/mcp-server/tools
https://www.anthropic.com/news/model-c
raw_transcript: Okay, so MCP servers can chew through your contacts window before you even sent a single message. Sometimes half it just from tool definition. So in this video we're going to walk through 10 techniques that bring your numbers way down. Some of them are simple configuration changes and other are more advanced patterns like code execution and programmatic tool calling. The best ones can cut your token usage by up to 98% which means cheaper, faster and more accurate agents. The first approach is code execution. So instead of loading all the definitions of all the tools, you give the agent a sandbox environment where it treats the MCP server as a file system. This was introduced by Anthropic and Cloudflare published the same idea a few weeks earlier under the name code mode. So this is a pattern two of the major AI infrastructure companies converged on independently. It works as each tool corresponds to a file. If you have a Google Drive and Salesforce as MCP servers, you would have a folder for each one with a TypeScript file per tool. The agent discovers what's available by exploring the file system and only reads the specific files it needs for the task in front of it. This does progressive disclosure and substantially reduces the number of tokens in the context window. The example in Anthropic's post is moving a Google Drive document into Salesforce where the original approach with direct tool calls would push around 150,000 tokens through the context. With code execution, only the result of the code reaches the model around 2,000 tokens which is roughly a 98% reduction. There are also some nice secondary benefits. The agent can filter large data sets in code before any of it ever reaches the model. You can write loops and conditionals as code instead of going back to the model between every step. And for sensitive data, intermediate results stay in execution environment. Things like emails or phone numbers can flow through your workflow without ever entering the context window. This is the most powerful approach in this video but it's only the highest complexity because you do need a real sandbox with proper isolation and resource limits. Okay, a similar approach is to use tool search tools from Anthropic. In this case, the agent has access to a few basic tools and a search tool. And when it needs more, it does a search through the catalog pretty much the same way Claude could does file search. This enables the agent to work with thousands of tools by dynamically discovering and loading them on demand. There are two variants documented. One uses regex and the other one uses BM25 which is just a natural language ranking. You enable it by adding the search tool to your tool list and then on every tool you don't want loaded immediately, you set default loading to true. The numbers Anthropic published here were pretty significant. A typical multi-server setup adds up to around 55,000 tokens of tool definitions before any work starts. The tool search reduces this by over 85%. Also helps with selection accuracy which the docs note degrades once you pass 30 to 50 tools. Now, both of these approaches are great but they kind of move away from the original promise of MCP itself which is that the server tells the client what tools are available. Another approach is scope loading where you create a group of tools based on their similarities. So let's say you have an MCP server that lets you access different kinds of data, e-commerce, finance, social media and so on. You create groups of these tools and dynamically load only the specific group you need. This approach was introduced by Bright Data in their MCP server. This is the open source MCP server they ship and a huge thanks to Bright Data for making this video possible through their sponsorship. Bright Data unlocks web data access and their MCP server has more than 60 tools across 11 groups. The way you use it is that you specify which group or groups you want when you configure the connection. You can do this through the URL with a groups parameter or as an environment variable in your local config. You can also combine multiple groups in the same session. This gives you a very efficient token utilization. You only pay for the tools you actually need and the implementation is open source so you can use the same pattern in your own MCP servers as well. Now, you can take this one step further. So instead of defining a group, you can actually define the specific tools you want available for a given session. In the Bright Data setup, this is tools environment variable. You list the exact tool names you want and those are the only ones loaded. This results in even more token reduction. If your application only needs four tools out of the 60 available, you load four tools and pay for it four tools only. The catch is you need to know what tools exist on the server to choose from them. So this is more for when you have already done the discovery and you're locking down your production agent to a specific job. Another approach is dynamic context loading. This is inspired by Claude skills which dynamically loads information and presents it to the agent only when needed. The idea is that you have three levels of disclosure and the agent walks down them as it figures out what it needs. So in the first level, you simply tell the agent what MCP servers are available. In the second level, once the agent decides a server is relevant, it gets the list of tools in the server with one line summary. And the third level is when it actually pulls the name, description and input schema for a specific tool. This approach can not only save you token, only the information that's actually relevant ends up there. And the nice thing is that you can combine this with the group tools or the custom tools approach. They work at different layers. And speaking of skills, Bright Data actually ships their own skill package using the same skill.md format that Anthropic introduced. There are five skills in there. Each skill is a folder with skill.md file which has a YAML formatter and plain markdown instructions. This works across more than 40 coding agents through what's called the Open Agent Skill Ecosystem. Another approach is programmatic tool calling. This is a separate Anthropic API feature and it sits next to the code execution in the docs. The idea is that you let Claude write code that calls your tool as Python functions and current and crucially, the intermediate results from those tool calls don't enter the model's context window. Only the final code output does. The model goes from reasoning over hundreds of kilobytes to reasoning over a handful of lines. You enable it by adding the code execution tool to your tools list and marking each callable tool with allowed callers set to code execution. The Anthropic docs note that on agentic search benchmarks like browse, comp and deep search QA, adding programmatic tool calling on top of a basic search was the key factor that fully unlocked agent performance. One caveat to know about, tools provided through an MCP connectors cannot currently be called programmatically. So this composes well with tools you define directly in your application but there's a gap with MCP today that Anthropic flags for future work. Another approach is a layered MCP server design. In this case, you have three layers between the LLM and the actual underlying MCP implementation. So discovery, planning and execution are the three layers. Think of this as a sub-agent design where most of the actions happen inside the sub-agent. So the context of the orchestrator, the original agent, stays pristine. It generates inputs and only gets results back. This is more of an architectural pattern than feature you turn on and it really only makes sense at scale when you have a lot of underlying MCP servers or when different teams own different tools and you want a clean interface in front of them. For most setups, the simpler approach further up are enough. Now, you can also optimize both the input and output tokens. So as we saw with the group tools, you already get a lot of optimization on the input data. However, if you're looking at web data or any data that has formatting, usually those tools or web search results will return with formatting. These are pretty smart systems by now. So you can potentially strip down the markdowns or any sort of formatics and just pass the plain text results. This approach alone can save you meaningful number of tokens on every response. And if you go further with light parsing on Google search results, taking just the top organic results and dropping the ads and the related searches, you can actually save even more. The exact numbers depends on the page, but the principle holds. So, this is something practical to consider if you're implementing an MCP server that has a lot of tools that interact with documents or web pages that have formatting in them. There is one more output optimization that's worth covering on its own because it's different kind of saving. It's called TOON, Token Oriented Object Notation. The problem TOON solves is that the JSON repeats every field name on every record. So, if you have a list of three products, you have IDs, names, price three times each. TOON declares the field names once at the top and then streams the row like CSV. So, instead of repeating the key, you just have the values. The general claim is 30 to 60% token reduction compared to standard JSON. The catch is that the TOON works on flat uniform data. If you have deeply nested structures like LinkedIn profiles with nested experience and education and skills, TOON loses its edge because the tabular layout doesn't apply. Now, in general, you shouldn't be relying on a single approach. Ideally, you want to stack these together to get the most out of your context window. In practice, tool groups at the connection layer to scope what's loaded, tool search for tools that do not fit cleanly into group, programmatic tool calling for any multi-step workflow, and output stripping on every tool that returns formatted data. And TOON encoding on top of it for any flat tabular response. If you're feeling ambitious, code execution with MCP replaces direct tool calls entirely. The beauty is that all of this is open source. Especially, the Bright Data MCP server is on GitHub under MIT license. You also get 5,000 requests per month on free tier, which is pretty generous for prototyping. So, do let me know which approach you're using with your own MCP server tools. And please share any tips you have. I hope you found this video useful. Thanks for watching, and as always, see you in the next one.

analysis.summary: 该视频讲解了10种通过MCP服务器减少AI代理令牌使用的方法，包括代码执行、工具搜索、范围加载、动态上下文加载、编程工具调用及输出优化等，可节省高达98%的令牌，实现更便宜、更快速、更准确的代理。

Adam Bridges, Benji Rabhan, B Shang, Cameron Navor, Charles Ian Norman Venn, Christian Ahlin, Eric T, Fred R, Gordon Child, Juan Benet, Michael Tedder, Owen Skarpness, Rich
raw_transcript: Let's see what is going on here. This is me around 9:00 a.m. A bit wobbly, steps are unsure. Yep. That checks out. Now then, give me my fake badge. Thank you, sir. No one noticed. Now, let's proceed to the next step of my mastermind plan. Yes, let's eat all of their food. Wait, they noticed. Okay. Proceed to the next step. What was that? Oh, yes. Run. Now, jokes aside, look at that. Oh, yes. Please mow my lawn. That is excellent. Now, rake the leaves. Perfect. Hey, don't slack off. That's my job. Okay. So, what is going on here? Well, let's start with the good news. This is a new teleoperated robot controller and more. They call it Sonic. Good name. Now, the work here is not the robot, but the software controlling the robot, at least in this footage. Watch until the end and you might get surprised. So, teleoperation means there is a human performing these movements and the robot is able to understand these motions and then translate them to a bunch of joint positions in 3D space. That's kind of insane if you think about it. But, it will just get better and better as we continue the video. So, before you ask, yes, it can do kung fu, provided that you can do kung fu. It understands whole body movement. So, you can get it to crawl into some space you don't want to go to and that is super useful. People are already using robots for that. Why? Well, chiefly for exploring under-explored and dangerous areas. This means tons of useful applications. For instance, a variant of this could help save humans stuck under rubble or perhaps later even explore other planets without putting humans at risk. But, that is still nothing because this is a multimodal system. Meaning that the input can be almost anything. So, you say that I don't have to pretend to mow the lawn to actually get it to mow the lawn because where's the fun in that? Well, you just tell it to do that. Can you? Well, currently, for simpler tasks like moving around and behaving like a monkey, yes, you can. Absolutely incredible. And I love how expressive it is. You can ask it to walk happily, stealthily, or like an injured person. And, you know, just the fact that it is stable and does not fall is remarkable. Previously, even in simple characters in simulated worlds, you needed thousands and thousands of tries to teach them to just be able to walk without falling. And now, this. This is a huge leap forward. Wow. But, it gets better. We said multimodal. Yep, that means the input can also be music. About that, I'll show you the dancing, but not the music because of YouTube reasons. But, I'll put a link in the description where you can check it out. And we haven't even talked about the most insane part of the whole thing. Now, hold on to your papers, fellow scholars, because this runs with about 42 million parameters. That is a neural network so simple, it can run so easily on your phone, it barely notices. It may even run on your toaster these days. That size is absolutely nothing. >> [sighs and gasps] >> This is an incredible achievement. Okay, but how? How is that even possible? Dear fellow scholars, this is Two Minute Papers with Dr. Karoly Zsolnai-Feher. Well, first, it looked at a hundred million frames of human motion to understand what we do and how we do it. The incredible thing is that this system does not require human-made action labels. So, we don't have to explain our movements. It just watches the raw motions and figures out how to transition between tasks without any unnatural pauses. So then, your multimodal input goes in, a video of you, your voice, music, a motion generator turns these into human motion and the human encoder processes it into a latent space and then a quantizer converts it to universal tokens. Once again, universal tokens. That is the key, you'll see a bit later. Then, the decoder translates these tokens into motor commands. But, >> [gasps and sighs] >> there is a big problem. Learning to convert one to the other is super hard. First of all, robots do not work like humans. That is one of the fundamental challenges. So, if the user commands you to turn around, it should be turning around. Okay. Sure. But, how fast exactly? You don't want to turn 180° too quickly because you would fall apart. To solve this, in their research paper, they propose what they call a root trajectory spring model. This looks for quick motions and dampens them so the robot does not get injured. Yes. Robots can get injured, too, which is kind of hilarious. Now, there is an exponential term there as a function of time. What is that? That is a physical break. As time increases, this term rapidly shrinks to zero, which forces the whole mathematical expression to decay smoothly. >> [snorts] >> This serves two goals. One, the robot does not injure itself. That's good. Two, it will settle at a target position without oscillating back and forth forever. Nice. It basically have this autonomous process to making sure domain of procedure knowledge is autonomously saved and maintained. So every single conversation history will be saved to this local SQLite DB that can be searched and retrieved using session search. And if you need, they also have a way for you to plug into a semantic memory layer like Mem Zero or Qdrant. The main part agent is managing, apart from skill and the raw conversation history, are just these two file of memory.md and user.md. So you can see that they really try to push agent to just use skill as a way to maintain most of task knowledge. And they have similar type of async background process to extract memory. It is counting the number of agent turns. And only after 10 turns, if there's no memory extraction happened before, it will respond a new memory reviewer agent with a special prompt. Has the user review things about themselves, their persona, desire, preference, has the user express expectation about how you should behave. If so, save them to those two files. So this is how Hermes agent works. It basically have the hot memory that is autonomously extracted every 10 turns, as well as warm memory of all sorts of different skills that is again autonomously extracted every time when there's more than 10 steps. As well as large cold memory for conversation history, a semantic long-term DB that agent can search. And after going through this, you can basically map out how the different agents works and understand why Hermes agent feels just smarter because it has those async autonomous process across skill and memory creation updates, as well as a way for agent to search raw conversation log. And this kind of like the state-of-the-art implementation for you to build any kind of in-context self-learning aspect for your agent too. You basically use skill for capture domain knowledge, memory for facts, and a searchable and auditable raw history. And ideally have async process so we don't rely on agent or human to extract and maintain snapshot of knowledge. And if you're already using Open Claw, you actually don't have to change to Hermes agent to get this type of really good self-learning experience. There are different skills on the market that already available with plugin and enhance your Open Claw or Claw Code's memory and self-learning setup. And here are three skills that I tested and found a pretty novel approach. I put the table here that can take a look in the detail. Their setup is very similar to what we just discussed before. And they have pretty smart use of hooks to making sure this memory creation and updates are more followable. For example, they use this user prompt submit hook. Then they also have this post tool use hook after every bash command. They will check the result from bash command to see if they match with any error pattern. If it does introduce errors, they will again append a error detected reminder prompt as part of tool result. And for Open Claw, when it is bootstrapped, they also have the self-improving reminder.md file that is injected as part of the system prompt. You just give your company website, connect all your business data source and integrations. And most importantly, it remembers all the actions it ever took so it can it review and improve them next time. We're opening our access to member in AI Builder Club. analysis.summary: 视频分析了聊天机器人用户体验变差的原因，如Anthropic Opus 4.7。作者认为这源于与社交媒体类似的“劣质化”趋势，主要由成本优化、规避法律诉讼风险（导致模型被“削弱”），以及减少幻觉（导致模型过度批判用户）等商业与安全激励结构驱动，最终牺牲了用户体验。

title: What is going on with AI?
raw_transcript: So, a lot of you had this question, and that is basically like, "Dave, there's a lot of confusing and conflicting information out there. Can you help make sense of it?" And I asked the audience. I said, "Okay, guys, what are what are some of the like key ideas that you find conflicting?" And so, it kind of orbited around like the data centers and the AI bubble and the jobs and that sort of stuff. So, that's obviously kind of the the high cluster. And so, today what I want to talk about is two specific very concrete kind of areas or one is more concrete and the other is more narrative, rather. So, on the concrete side, let's talk about the data centers and the AI and that sort of stuff. So, you might have heard the the narrative that like, "Oh, AI is a bubble and data centers are getting canceled and that sort of thing." And then on the other side is the more narrative-driven side where one particular name came up multiple times and that is Cal Newport. Now, Cal Newport, if you're not familiar, is a tech writer and business writer. He's very popular, very famous. I haven't found much of his work com- particularly compelling. I'll get into that in a in a minute. But, let's just start with the data centers. So, at the highest level, the thing that you need to understand about data centers and the AI buildout overall is that when adjusted for inflation, the data center buildout and also as a percentage of GDP, the data center buildout is the second largest mega project that we have ever embarked on. It is bigger than everything except the Marshall Plan as a percentage of GDP. And it is the only one that is privately funded. Everything else has been a mega project. And so, when I say mega project, I mean like the Manhattan Project, the Apollo Program, the Interstate Highway System, those sorts of things. Every other mega project has been state-sponsored and state-directed. This is the only one that is private. So, of course, when there's that much investment, one of the one of the only mental models that people have is, "Okay, well, if private investment is too high, that's a bubble." You don't call it a bubble if it's the government saying, "Hey, we're going to build the Hoover Dam. We're going to build the the space program." Now, people did protest the space program. There that's one thing that kind of gets glossed over depending on which movie you're watching about the '60s and '70s because guess what was going on at that time? The Vietnam War, the Civil Rights Movement, and all kinds of stuff. America was a hot mess back then. So, if you watch stuff like The Right Stuff and other other TV shows and stuff about that era, it usually focuses just on NASA, just on the astronauts, and glosses over the protests. And a lot of people were not happy with how much money was being spent on NASA. So, it's one thing to protest and say, "This is a misallocation of resources." It's another thing when people say, "You know, this is a bubble. It's over-leveraged. Where's the return on investment?" And that sort of thing. Now, we have seen in the past massive massive investment. Probably one of the only other private buildouts that was this big was the railroads. Now, railroads took 15, 20 years to really cover the costs just one in the amount of time that it took to build them, but then once they're operating, they had to operate at an at a, you know, high enough margin to actually justify the expense. So, when we talk about like, "Okay, when private builds out something this big, whether it's data centers or GPUs or that sort of thing, the thing is is the data center doesn't expire. Data centers are there for 50-plus years. So, that investment is a capital asset that stays there and only becomes more valuable over time like most real estate does. So, when you look at a data center buildout from the lens of you're not buying a GPU cuz this is this is one thing that I think a lot of people don't differentiate. They say, "Oh, well, the GPUs are worthless in 2 years." Which isn't true. Yes, they depreciate. Yes, there's newer GPUs, but GPUs don't become worthless after 2 years. They don't stop working. It's just that that's no longer your highest performance cluster. And also like you can often resell them. And then also from a tax perspective, you get to amateurize that that expense. So, the GPUs not only are paying for themselves in those 2 years that you have them, but they're also a tax write-off. So, people that like complain about GPUs, it's like, "Tell me that you know nothing about CapEx versus OpEx without telling me that you know nothing about CapEx versus OpEx." Like seriously. And so, this is this kind of leads into the criticism that I have of people like Cal Newport, which I'll get into in a second. But but the TLDR is that there's a lot of nuance that doesn't make it into the academic papers. That if you're an industry operator, that you just know these things and you forget that other people don't know them. So, we'll get into that. Excuse [clears throat] me. We'll get into that in just a second. But, when you have this level of, you know, investment, private, public, whatever, it is producing durable assets. So, the thing is is like people might have complained about, you know, the Marshall Plan or the Interstate Highway System or you know, the railroad buildout or whatever else, but there's artifacts that are being created. And I don't mean artifact like, you know, stuff that you dig up, you know, millennia later. What I mean is is an actual financial artifact, an asset that is there that gets used. The same thing happened with the internet buildout. So, people are like, "Oh, the internet was overbuilt." And you know, people are always reaching for analogies and they say like, "Ah, well, you know, the data center buildout is like the internet buildout where they're just building and they're hoping the customers would come." And it's like, "Well, yeah, but you know, a lot of the companies that built it out went under and only after 10 or 15 years did we really make use of all that internet infrastructure that they put into place. Okay. Even if it takes 10 or 15 years for it to fully justify the cost, that's not like there was an internet pause. There was no great internet pause from 2003 to 2012 while everything caught up. 2003 when the dot-com bubble burst to 2012 was still a very productive era in terms of e-commerce and things spinning up. It just wasn't at the fever pitch that that it was before. And this is another thing where people have tried to compare the AI buildout to like a tulip craze. The thing is, the tulip craze or tulip mania, the tulips have no value. They have no intrinsic value. Do you can't eat them. You can't resell them. They expire very quickly and that sort of thing. But, every time you look at a capitalist buildout, you end up with a durable asset. You end up with something that continues to be useful. analysis.summary: 该视频分析了当前围绕AI的困惑，主要探讨了两点：一是AI数据中心建设并非泡沫，而是私营主导的巨大基础建设项目，创造耐用资产；二是批判学术界对AI的评估，认为其研究方法过时、缺乏行业经验且发布周期缓慢，导致对AI的生产力提升和实际能力存在误解，强调行业内部人士的实时洞察更为重要。
analysis.speaker: David Shapiro

analysis.summary: 该视频探讨了AI领域的“错失恐惧症”（FOMO），并将其归结为金钱、地位、机遇和安全四个方面。作者分享了六种应对策略，包括认识到AI仍处于早期阶段、接受他人成功、持续自我定位、以历史为鉴、放弃零和竞争拥抱开源精神，以及断网休息以重置心态。

title: Opus 4.7 is here... upgrade or downgrade?
description:
Opus 4.7 is here and its the most interesting release from Anthropic

https://www.anthropic.com/news/claude-opus-4-7

My Dictation App: www.whryte.com
Website: https://engineerprompt.ai/
RAG Beyond Basics Course:
https://prompt-s-site.thinkific.com/courses/rag
Signup for Newsletter, localgpt: https://tally.so/r/3y9bb0

Let's Connect: 
🦾 Discord: https://discord.com/invite/t4eYQRUcXB
☕ Buy me a Coffee: https://ko-fi.com/promptengineering
|🔴 Patreon: https://www.patreon.com/PromptEngineering
💼Cons
raw_transcript: Okay, so Anthropic just released Claude Opus 4.7. And this seems to be a very interesting release. It surpasses Opus 4.6 on a number of key benchmarks, especially when it comes to coding. It seems to have a better instruction-following capabilities. Also, multimodal understanding capabilities have improved substantially. It's able to use files as a memory system much better. And it seems to be trained for self-verification during code generation. They also introduced a few new changes and parameters in the API and platform. Now, even though on benchmarks it's surpassing GPT-54 and Opus 4.6, still I would say this release is pretty interesting. The first one is timing. So, they dropped the model at around 7:30 a.m., which is very, very unusual for Anthropic. A potential reason is that probably we're going to see an OpenAI later in the morning. Now, unlike the previous releases, they basically announced it on X with a simple thread without any demos or anything. So, it definitely feels like a rushed release. They recently released a preview of Metis. And with this release they're directly competing the two models as well, which is pretty interesting. So, the first thing is very clear. Metis preview is a much stronger model overall. Opus 4.7 is better at agentic coding compared to Opus 4.6. However, if you look at the agentic tool use benchmarks, this is where the difference does not seem to be that high, especially for agentic search. Scale 2 use, agentic computer use is actually pretty close to Medusa. Now, in the blog post, they said that last week when they released Medusa preview, the release itself was pretty limited. Now, for Opus 4.7, the cyber capabilities are not as advanced as those of Medusa preview, and that's why I think Anthropic is comfortable releasing it. Now, they're saying that we're releasing Opus 4.7 with safeguards that automatically detect and block requests that indicate prohibited or high-risk cyber issues or uses. But, this is a same class of model as Opus 4.6 because the API pricing is exactly the same as the previous generation. Now, some interesting things if you're thinking of using this model. So, first is instruction following. According to Anthropic, Opus 4.7 takes instructions literally, which is different than the previous iteration, where the previous models interpreted instructions loosely or skips part entirely. If you use the same instructions for Opus 4.7, you might see some unexpected results. And that's why they say that users should retune their prompts and harness accordingly. Now, this is one of the issues that we have had with large language models, that with every iteration, you as a user have to really go back and look at whether it's going to follow the instructions the same way as the previous iteration, and that also sometimes involve readjusting your harness around the model. Second, according to Anthropic, it has improved multi-modal, which means it has better vision for high-resolution images. Now, multi-modal is an important part of any agentic workflow. So, if you're working with documents, you're going to see a lot of multi-modal data. So, in an agentic use case, it surpasses Opus 4 6 substantially on multi-modal agentic use case. And you can also see a substantial difference between the performance depending on the resolution of the input data. Now, keep in mind, high-resolution images will be more accurate, but they'll incur more tokens as well, and hence it's going to be more costly. Now, another thing that they have highlighted is that Opus 4 6 is better at using file system-based memory. This is important, especially for coding agents. Claude Code, for example, relies completely on files for its memory system. So, it seems like they are going in that direction and really focusing on uh file system as a memory, but rather than using semantic similarity uh type of approaches that other providers are exploring. Now, based on the benchmarks that they have provided, it seems to have better document reasoning, and I think this is improved by the fact that it has better multi-modal reasoning capabilities now. Also, seems to have the same context window of 1 million token, but with a relatively better reasoning across that context window. Now, an important benchmark that they have added is the Vending Bench 2. Seems to have a much better long-term coherence, and it's much more profitable compared to Opus 4 6. Okay, then one interesting aspect of the benchmarks that they have shared. So, when it comes to multimodal understanding, on their internal benchmarks, it sees a substantial difference. On external ones, you might not see a big of a difference. Now, one thing, if you're going to be moving or migrating from Opus 4 6 to 4 7, there are a couple of things that you need to consider. The first one is the tokenizer. So, Opus 4 7 uses an updated tokenizers that improve how the model processes text. The trade-off is that the same input can map to more tokens, which is roughly 1. or can be up to 1.35 times, depending on the content type. Second, Opus 4 7 thinks more at a higher effort levels, particularly on later turns in the agentic system. This improves its reliability on hard problems, but does mean it produces more output tokens and hence we're going to be burning through our rate limits much faster. So, this is something that you definitely want to keep an eye on if you're going to be moving from Opus 4 6 to 4 7. Okay, so this brings us to some new settings that they have introduced. So, Opus 4 6 introduces extra high effort between high and max. And by default in Claude code, the effort level is now extra high. If you go to Claude code, the default for Opus 4 6 is medium, which showed a degradation in performance where a lot of people are complaining. So, I think Anthropic is trying to address that with a default of extra high. However, having this default also means that you're going to to burning through your tokens a lot quicker. Now, on Cloud API, they have added support for high-resolution images and they are also launching task budgets in public beta, giving developers a way to guide Cloud's token spend to so it can prioritize work across the longer terms. They're also introducing something called ultra review, which is a slash command that produces a dedicated review session that reads through changes and find bugs and design issues that a careful reviewer would catch. So, one thing, if you are going to be using this for coding, you need to make sure to set the proper effort level because setting the proper effort level is going to be helpful. However, be mindful of the extra tokens that you're going to be burning through in the process. Okay, so one thing which I noticed at the end is that Sweet Bench Multimodal, they say we use an internal implementation for both Opus 4.6 and 4.7. Scores are not directly comparable to public leaderboards scores. So, there seems to be some benching tricks happening in here. Anyways, this was a quick update. I do expect that there's going to be an OpenAI release this morning. If that happens, I am going to create and put out another quick video. Thanks for watching and as always, see you in the next one.

analysis.summary: 视频探讨“后劳动经济学”，定义为AI和自动化使人类劳动不再是经济产出制约的经济体系。它追溯了该概念的起源，将AI视为通用技术，并详细阐述了其经济影响，如通货紧缩、去货币化和潜在的“通货紧缩死亡螺旋”。它分析了对家庭收入、政府收入的后果，并提出了全民基本收入和基于资本收入的解决方案。
analysis.speaker: David Shapiro

title: Normalize Post Labor Economics
description:
KICKSTARTER FOR LABOR/ZERO: https://www.kickstarter.com/projects/daveshap/labor-zero

UNIVERSAL HIGH INCOME: https://github.com/daveshap/UniversalHighIncome
raw_transcript: Post-labor economics is meant to build on and extend neoliberalism so that left versus right, they can argue about specifics, about specific priorities or specific programs, but the idea is that within 5 to 10 years, I want every politician to implicitly be on board with post-labor economics in the same way that today they're implicitly on board with neoliberalism.

title: Anthropic Is Building a Super App

analysis.summary: 视频主要介绍了Anthropic对其Claude桌面体验的重大更新，特别是围绕Claude Code的重新设计，使其成为一个“超级应用”。重点功能包括并行面板、内置浏览器预览、专用终端、PR管理以及云端运行的“例行任务”（Routines）功能。最后也提到了Anthropic定价模式可能转向基于代币
